<colgroup>
<col style="width: 50%" />
<col style="width: 50%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td width="15%"><strong>ApplicationData</strong></td>
<td>Application-level data that will be carried by a fasp session.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td width="15%"> </td>
<td><table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>Cookie</strong></td>
<td>An arbitrary field used for application-specific requirements. This field can be used as a placeholder for a unique reference. The difference with PrivateData Key is that the Cookie is known to both the local (Client) and the remote (Server), while the PrivateData Key is known to the local (Client).</td>
<td>string</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Token</strong></td>
<td>This field is used for token-based authorization.</td>
<td>string</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 50%" />
<col style="width: 50%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td width="15%"><strong>RemoteLocation</strong></td>
<td>Identifies the remote endpoint and file locations involved in the transfer.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td width="15%"> </td>
<td><table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 50%" />
<col style="width: 50%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>System</strong></td>
<td>Identifies a system on the network.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td> </td>
<td><table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 33%" />
<col style="width: 33%" />
<col style="width: 33%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>Address</strong></td>
<td>IP address or host name of the system.</td>
<td>string</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Port</strong></td>
<td>SSH port over which the transfer will be initiated.</td>
<td><p>unsignedShort.</p>
<p>Default 22</p></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>UserName</strong></td>
<td>A user name used to authenticate on the system.</td>
<td>string</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Password</strong></td>
<td>A password used to authenticate on the system.</td>
<td>string</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Authentication</strong></td>
<td>Specifies the parameters used to authenticate on the remote system. When using SSH key authentication, make sure that the key file on the node is not a shared key. On the node computer, the key file should be a "private" key in the specified user account.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td> </td>
<td><table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 50%" />
<col style="width: 50%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>Methods</strong></td>
<td>SSH methods to use for authentication</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td> </td>
<td><table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>Method</strong></td>
<td>Specifies the authentication mechanism. A single or up to 3 instances of the Method elements can be nested in the Methods element.</td>
<td><p>Possible values: { 'Public Key'|'Password'|'Token' }</p></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>KeyPath</strong></td>
<td>The path to SSH key if Public key authentication method is used</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>KeyPassphrase</strong></td>
<td>A string denoting the passphrase for the key</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Files</strong></td>
<td>Paths to the transfer contents on the remote host.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td> </td>
<td><table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 33%" />
<col style="width: 33%" />
<col style="width: 33%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>Path</strong></td>
<td>A transfer file path. For user name with document root path configured, the path must expressed relative to the user document root path.<br />
A single or many instances of the Path elements can be nested in the Files element.</td>
<td>string</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>RootPath</strong></td>
<td>Path prefix (since version 3.3).</td>
<td>string</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Proxy</strong></td>
<td>If this parameter is defined, the transfer will use fasp-proxy. Specifies the Destination Network Address Translation (DNAT or secure DNAT) parameters to use a fasp-proxy for the transfer: user name, password and proxy URL.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td> </td>
<td><table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>Url</strong></td>
<td>string. Specifies the address of the Aspera high-speed proxy server in the form of: <strong>dnat(s)://[user[:password]@]server[:port]</strong>. Default ports for DNAT and DNATS protocols are 9091 and 9092. </td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>User</strong></td>
<td>string. Not needed if the Url element contains the user name.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Password</strong></td>
<td>string. Not needed if the Url element contains the password.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 50%" />
<col style="width: 50%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td width="15%"><strong>LocalLocation</strong></td>
<td>Identifies local file paths involved in the transfer.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td width="15%"> </td>
<td><table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 50%" />
<col style="width: 50%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>System</strong></td>
<td>Identifies a user in the local system on the network. Optional.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td> </td>
<td><table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>UserName</strong></td>
<td>A user name used to authenticate on the system.</td>
<td>string</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Password</strong></td>
<td>A password used to authenticate on the system. Optional.</td>
<td>string</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Files</strong></td>
<td>Paths to the transfer contents on the local host.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td> </td>
<td><table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 33%" />
<col style="width: 33%" />
<col style="width: 33%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>Path</strong></td>
<td>A transfer file path. For user name with document root path configured, the path must expressed relative to the user document root path.<br />
A single or many instances of the Path elements can be nested in the Files element.</td>
<td>string</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>RootPath</strong></td>
<td>Path prefix (since version 3.3).</td>
<td>string</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 50%" />
<col style="width: 50%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td width="15%"><strong>FileParameters</strong></td>
<td>Specifies file-related parameters pertaining to the transfer.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td width="15%"> </td>
<td><table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 33%" />
<col style="width: 33%" />
<col style="width: 33%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>CreatePath</strong></td>
<td>Specifies whether or not destination directories should be created if they don't exist.</td>
<td><p>Possible values: { 'Yes'|'No' }.</p>
<p>Default: No</p></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>ResumeCheck</strong></td>
<td>Specifies the mechanism to use when resuming partially transferred files.</td>
<td><p>Possible values: { 'None'|'Attributes'|'Sparse Checksum'|'Full Checksum' }</p>
<p>Default: Sparse Checksum</p></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>ManifestPolicy</strong></td>
<td>Manifest policy. It states if manifest files should be generated or not for transfers.</td>
<td><p>Possible values: { 'None'|'Text' }</p>
<p>Default: None</p></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>ManifestPath</strong></td>
<td>Manifest file path. If this property is set, while the ManifestPolicy is not explicitly defined, the system infers that the value of the ManifestPolicy property is "Text", and will generate manifest files.</td>
<td>string</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Protection</strong></td>
<td>Specifies if encryption at rest will be applied to the transfer. A passphrase will be set to secure the process.</td>
<td><p>Possible values: { 'None'|'Encrypt'|'Decrypt' }</p>
<p>Default: None</p></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Passphrase</strong></td>
<td>A passphrase to use for encryption at rest.</td>
<td>string</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>PreCalculateJobSize</strong></td>
<td>Specifies whether the aggregate size of all files in the job is to be calculated before starting the transfer.</td>
<td><p>Possible values: { 'Yes'|'No' }.</p>
<p>Default: No</p></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>RemoveAfterTransfer</strong></td>
<td>Specifies whether the source files are deleted after the transfer. All folders in the source path will still remain. Also refer to RemoveEmptyDirectories.</td>
<td><p>Possible values: { 'Yes'|'No' }.</p>
<p>Default: No</p></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>RemoveEmptyDirectories</strong></td>
<td>Specifies whether empty directories in the source path are deleted after the transfer.</td>
<td><p>Possible values: { 'Yes'|'No' }.</p>
<p>Default: No</p></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>SymbolicLinks</strong></td>
<td>Specifies symbolic links are handled during the transfer.</td>
<td><p>Possible values: { 'Follow'|'Copy'|'Skip'|'Copy+Force' }</p>
<p>Default: Follow</p></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>PreserveTimestamps</strong></td>
<td>Specifies whether source timestamps are preserved at the destination.</td>
<td><p>Possible values: { 'Yes'|'No' }.</p>
<p>Default: No</p></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>ReadBlockSize</strong></td>
<td>Specifies the read block size, in bytes.</td>
<td>unsignedInt.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>WriteBlockSize</strong></td>
<td>Specifies the write block size, in bytes.</td>
<td>unsignedInt.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Overwrite</strong></td>
<td>Specifies the transfer behavior if a content to transfer already exists at destination.</td>
<td><p>Possible values: { 'Always'|'Never'|'Diff'|'Older'|'DiffAndOlder' }</p>
<p>Default: Diff</p></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td rowspan="2"><strong>Exclude</strong></td>
<td colspan="2">Specifies path exclusion patterns. Paths that match excluded patterns are not transferred.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td colspan="2"><table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>Pattern</strong></td>
<td>Exclusion pattern. A single or up to 16 instances of the Pattern elements can be nested in the Exclude element.</td>
<td>string</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>SourceBase</strong></td>
<td>If this option is utilized, ascp will strip the source base path, while preserving the rest of the directory structure. For example, consider the "clips" directory with a file: /clips/outgoing/file1 and we want to transfer file1 within the "outgoing" folder (but not the "outgoing" folder, itself). By setting SourceBase element to /clips/outgoing/, the transferred file will appear in the destination directory as (docroot)/file1.</td>
<td width="20">string</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 50%" />
<col style="width: 50%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td width="15%"> <br />
<strong>LinkParameters</strong></td>
<td>Specifies link-related parameters pertaining to the transfer.
<p>Link capacity can be detected automatically by using &lt;AutoDetectCapacity&gt; property.</p>
<p>Link capacity can also be set manually by using &lt;RemoteCapacity&gt; and or &lt;LocalCapacity&gt;:</p>
<ul>
<li>If &lt;RemoteCapacity&gt; is not defined, &lt;LocalCapacity&gt; is used as the link capacity.</li>
<li>&lt;RemoteCapacity&gt; is used as link capacity when its defined value is less than the value of &lt;LocalCapacity&gt;.</li>
<li>If none of &lt;RemoteCapacity&gt; and &lt;LocalCapacity&gt; is defined, the bandwidth cap will limit the transfer rates.<br />
 </li>
</ul></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td> </td>
<td><table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 33%" />
<col style="width: 33%" />
<col style="width: 33%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>RemoteCapacity</strong></td>
<td> </td>
<td>unsignedInt</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>LocalCapacity</strong></td>
<td> </td>
<td>unsignedInt</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>AutoDetectCapacity</strong></td>
<td>Indicates whether or not fasp should automatically detect the link capacity during transfer. Performing automatic detection allows rates to be set at a percentage of the measured value. When automatic bandwidth discovery is enabled (&lt;AutoDetectCapacity&gt; set to Yes) and the rates expressed as percentage (&lt;MinimumRateAsPercent&gt; or &lt;TargetRateAsPercent&gt; set to Yes), then the rates will be a percentage of the measured bandwidth. Otherwise, rates must be specified in Kbps.</td>
<td><p>Possible values: { 'Yes'|'No' }.</p>
<p>Default: No</p></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 50%" />
<col style="width: 50%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td width="15%"><strong>RateParameters</strong></td>
<td>Specifies rate-related parameters pertaining to the transfer.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td width="15%"> </td>
<td><table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 33%" />
<col style="width: 33%" />
<col style="width: 33%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>Policy</strong></td>
<td>Specifies the rate policy the transfer should utilize.</td>
<td><p>Possible values: { 'Fixed'|'Fair'|'Trickle' }</p>
<p>Default: Fixed</p></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>TargetRate</strong></td>
<td>The target rate of data transmission, in Kbps or %. If &lt;TargetRateAsPercent&gt; is set to Yes, this value will be interpreted as a percentage and should be set to values between 0 and 100, inclusive. The target rate cannot exceed the link capacity and the &lt;BandwidthCap&gt;.</td>
<td><p>unsignedInt.</p>
<p>Default: 10000</p></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>TargetRateAsPercent</strong></td>
<td>Indicates whether or not the value of &lt;TargetRate&gt; should be interpreted as a percentage (of the link capacity, see &lt;LinkParameters&gt; or the measured bandwidth). When automatic bandwidth discovery is enabled (&lt;AutoDetectCapacity&gt; set to Yes) and the rate as percentage is enabled (&lt;TargetRateAsPercent&gt; set to Yes), then the target rate will be a percentage of the measured bandwidth.</td>
<td><p>Possible values: { 'Yes'|'No' }.</p>
<p>Default: No</p></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>MinimumRate</strong></td>
<td>The minimum rate of data transmission, in Kbps or %. If &lt;MinimumRateAsPercent&gt; is set to Yes, this value will be interpreted as a percentage and should be set to values between 0 and 100, inclusive. The minimum rate cannot exceed the target rate, the link capacity and the &lt;BandwidthCap&gt;.</td>
<td><p>unsignedInt.</p>
<p>Default: 0</p></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>MinimumRateAsPercent</strong></td>
<td>Indicates whether or not the value of &lt;MinimumRate&gt; should be interpreted as a percentage (of the link capacity, see &lt;LinkParameters&gt; or the measured bandwidth). When automatic bandwidth discovery is enabled (&lt;AutoDetectCapacity&gt; set to Yes) and the rate as percentage is enabled (&lt;MinimumRateAsPercent&gt; set to Yes), then the minimum rate will be a percentage of the measured bandwidth.</td>
<td><p>Possible values: { 'Yes'|'No' }.</p>
<p>Default: No</p></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>BandwidthCap</strong></td>
<td>A cap on data transfer rates, in Kbps. A value of 10,000,000 indicates that no bandwidth cap will be applied.</td>
<td><p>unsignedInt.</p>
<p>Default: 10000000</p></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Priority</strong></td>
<td>Priority when sharing virtual bandwidth cap. 1 for higher priority, 2 for regular.</td>
<td><p>unsignedInt.</p>
<p>Possible values: {1, 2}</p>
<p>Default: 2</p></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 50%" />
<col style="width: 50%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td width="15%"><strong>ChannelParameters</strong></td>
<td>Specifies channel-related parameters pertaining to the transfer.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td width="15%"> </td>
<td><table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 33%" />
<col style="width: 33%" />
<col style="width: 33%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>Port</strong></td>
<td>UDP port over which data will be transported.</td>
<td><p>unsignedShort.</p>
<p>Default: 33001</p></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>DatagramSize</strong></td>
<td>The datagram size fasp will use when transmitting data. If this property is set, while the &lt;AutoDetectPathMTU&gt; is not explicitly defined, the system infers that the value of the &lt;AutoDetectPathMTU&gt; property is "No", and uses the datagram size value provided in this element.</td>
<td><p>unsignedInt.</p>
<p>Default: 1492</p></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>AutoDetectPathMTU</strong></td>
<td>Indicates whether or not fasp should automatically detect the path MTU (maximum transmission unit).</td>
<td><p>Possible values: { 'Yes'|'No' }.</p>
<p>Default: Yes</p></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 50%" />
<col style="width: 50%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td width="15%"><p><strong>SecurityParameters</strong></p></td>
<td>Specifies security-related parameters pertaining to the transfer.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td width="15%"> </td>
<td><table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 33%" />
<col style="width: 33%" />
<col style="width: 33%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>EncryptionCipher</strong></td>
<td>Specifies the encryption cipher used to encrypt data during transfer.</td>
<td><p>Possible values: { 'None'|'AES-128' }</p>
<p>Default: None</p></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 50%" />
<col style="width: 50%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td width="15%"><strong>RetryParameters</strong></td>
<td>Specifies retry-related parameters pertaining to the transfer. Retry parameters in user preferences will be applied if this element is not defined.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td width="15%"> </td>
<td><table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>Count</strong></td>
<td>Number of transfer attempts.</td>
<td>unsignedInt</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>BaseInterval</strong></td>
<td>Base time interval, in seconds. The base time interval specifies the base time that will elapse between a failed transfer and a retry attempt. After each subsequent failure, the time interval will be doubled, up to the maximum value as specified by &lt;MaximumInterval&gt;. Setting &lt;BaseInterval&gt; and &lt;MaximumInterval&gt; to the same value, effectively indicates a constant time interval between retry attempts.</td>
<td>unsignedInt</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>MaximumInterval</strong></td>
<td>Maximum time interval, in seconds. The maximum time interval limits the increasing of the time interval that occurs as a result of multiple, subsequent failures.</td>
<td>unsignedInt</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 50%" />
<col style="width: 50%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td width="15%"><strong>PrivateData</strong></td>
<td>Application defined private data. This field can be used by a client application as a placeholder for a local (unique) reference. It differs from the Cookie in that the Cookie is known to both the local (Client) and the remote (Server), while the PrivateData Key is known to the local (Client).</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td width="15%"> </td>
<td><table data-border="1" data-cellspacing="0" data-cellpadding="5">
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>Key</strong></td>
<td>The local reference key</td>
<td>string</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

##### Example Job Order

This example describes a Job Order (in this case a pull operation) in XML format.

##### [XML Format](https://developer.asperasoft.com/web/soap/job-submissions/182-job-definitions#)

``` markup
<Order>

    <Version>2</Version>

    <Agent>aspera.sdk.sample</Agent>

    <ApplicationData>

        <Cookie>Sample-Appl-jZUVvgLruAbKKebM4cJb_Ff6CSGqcW</Cookie>

        <Token>ATV7_jZUVvgLruAbKKebM4cJb_Ff6CSGqcW_lNtHDDytOSak_7VTA</Token>

    </ApplicationData>

    <Operation>Pull</Operation>

    <!-- other options: Push -->

    <RemoteLocation>

        <System>

            <Address>10.20.13.102</Address>

            <Port>22</Port>

            <UserName>user</UserName>

            <Password>password</Password>

        </System>

        <Authentication>

            <Methods>

                <Method>Password</Method>

                <Method>Public Key</Method>

            </Methods>

            <KeyPath>/home/userhome/.ssh/key</KeyPath>

            <KeyPassphrase>keypassphraseifany</KeyPassphrase>

        </Authentication>

        <Files>

            <Path>/home/user/file</Path>

        </Files>

        <Proxy>

            <Url>dnat://proxyaddr</Url>

            <!-- or dnat://proxyUser:proxyUserPass@proxyaddr -->

            <User>proxyUser</User>

            <Password>proxyUserPass</Password>

        </Proxy>

    </RemoteLocation>

    <LocalLocation>

        <System>

            <UserName>localUser</UserName>

            <Password>localPassword</Password>

        </System>

        <Files>

            <Path>/opt/localfiles/file</Path>

        </Files>

    </LocalLocation>

    <FileParameters>

        <CreatePath>Yes</CreatePath>

        <!-- other options: No -->

        <ResumeCheck>Sparse Checksum</ResumeCheck>

        <!-- other options: None, Full Checksum, Attributes -->

        <ManifestPath>/opt/tmp/manifests</ManifestPath>

        <Protection>Encrypt</Protection>

        <!-- other options: None, Decrypt -->

        <Passphrase>earpassphrase</Passphrase>

        <PreCalculateJobSize>Yes</PreCalculateJobSize>

        <!-- other options: No -->

        <RemoveAfterTransfer>No</RemoveAfterTransfer>

        <!-- other options: Yes -->

        <RemoveEmptyDirectories>No</RemoveEmptyDirectories>

        <!-- other options: Yes -->

        <SymbolicLinks>skip</SymbolicLinks>

        <!-- other options: follow, copy, copy+force -->

        <PreserveTimestamps>Yes</PreserveTimestamps>

        <!-- other options: No -->

        <Overwrite>Diff</Overwrite>

        <!-- other options: Always, Never, Older, DiffAndOlder -->

        <Exclude>

            <Pattern>*.pdf</Pattern>

        </Exclude>

        <SourceBase>src/dir</SourceBase>

    </FileParameters>

    <ChannelParameters>

        <Port>33001</Port>

        <DatagramSize>1500</DatagramSize>

        <AutoDetectPathMTU>Yes</AutoDetectPathMTU>

        <!-- other options: No -->

    </ChannelParameters>

    <LinkParameters>

        <RemoteCapacity>2000000</RemoteCapacity>

        <LocalCapacity>2000000</LocalCapacity>

        <AutoDetectCapacity>No</AutoDetectCapacity>

        <!-- other options: Yes -->

    </LinkParameters>

    <RateParameters>

        <Policy>Fair</Policy>

        <!-- other options: Fixed, Trickle -->

        <TargetRate>45000</TargetRate>

        <TargetRateAsPercent>No</TargetRateAsPercent>

        <!-- other options: Yes -->

        <MinimumRate>0</MinimumRate>

        <MinimumRateAsPercent>No</MinimumRateAsPercent>

        <!-- other options: Yes -->

        <BandwidthCap>2000000</BandwidthCap>

        <Priority>2</Priority>

        <!-- regular priority. Other option: 1 (higher priority)-->

    </RateParameters>

    <SecurityParameters>

        <EncryptionCipher>AES-128</EncryptionCipher>

        <!-- other options: None -->

    </SecurityParameters>

    <RetryParameters>

        <Count>3</Count>

        <BaseInterval>5</BaseInterval>

        <MaximumInterval>10</MaximumInterval>

    </RetryParameters>

</Order>
```

### Operating Instructions

- This action allows starting and monitoring Fasp Transfers using remote Aspera nodes. The plugin uses Aspera Central to initiate and control transfers, so Aspera Central must be configured to listen and accept connections from external nodes.
- For Aspera releases \< 3.4, the Aspera Node needs to have Reliable Query API enabled in aspera.conf with proper central_server configuration including address (0.0.0.0), port (40001), and persistent_store enabled.
- The plugin works with Enterprise Server, Connect Server, Faspex Server, and Point-to-Point configurations. For complete setup information and Central SOAP API understanding, visit the Aspera Developer Network.
- To retrieve failed transfer file lists, node user and password must be provided, and ascp version should be 3.6.1 or above for full functionality.
- Configure the split separator to 'None' when passing single files or directories with special characters to avoid confusion with file list separators (comma by default).
- Payload files are stored in `/opt/aspera/orchestrator/config/orchestrator/actions/fasp_transfers` and can be customized using embedded Ruby for dynamic transfer configurations.

### Dependencies

- Aspera Central SOAP API must be accessible and configured to accept external node connections.
- Reliable Query API must be enabled on Aspera servers for transfer monitoring (required for versions \< 3.4).
- Network connectivity between Orchestrator and Aspera transfer nodes with appropriate firewall configurations.
- Valid authentication credentials with sufficient permissions for transfer operations on target Aspera systems.
- ASCP version 3.6.1 or above for comprehensive failed file reporting capabilities.
- The aspweb user must have proper file permissions for payload files and any local transfer paths, as Orchestrator runs as aspweb instead of root.

### Troubleshooting

- If workflows break after plugin upgrades/downgrades, copy all files from `/opt/aspera/orchestrator/config/orchestrator/actions/fasp_transfers/payloads` to `/opt/aspera/orchestrator/config/orchestrator/actions/fasp_transfers` to correct payload issues.
- When downloading from Faspex servers connected to HSTS on different machines, map the `faspex_host` input parameter to the Faspex server address to avoid authorization token request issues.
- For transfers with special characters in file paths, set the split separator to 'None' to prevent misinterpretation as file list separators.
- Verify Aspera Central configuration accepts external connections if transfer initiation fails - check central_server settings in aspera.conf.
- Ensure proper authentication credentials and verify network connectivity if connection errors occur during transfer setup.
- Check ASCP version compatibility (3.6.1+) if failed file reporting is not working as expected.

### Faspex5 file processing

![Faspex5 file processing icon](icons/Faspex5FileProcessing.png){.plugin-icon}

This plugin integrates with Faspex5 servers to manage file processing workflows by retrieving lists of files awaiting validation and updating their processing status throughout the validation lifecycle.

| Version | Comment |
|----|----|
| 0.0.5 | Improved error handling during reauthentication and API operations |
| 0.0.4 | Updated the label from Keep ongoing to Continue Monitoring |
| 0.0.3 | Updated the label from Keep ongoing to Continue Monitoring |
| 0.0.2 | Fix issues with error message as optional input and node saving |
| 0.0.1 | This is based on AsperaOTFV version 0.0.1 |

### How It Works

- Integrates with the Faspex 5 server to process files requiring validation or status updates.
- Supports two main operations: retrieves lists of files awaiting processing, and updates file processing status.
- Authenticates with Faspex 5 using SSH private keys and API client credentials.
- Provides continuous monitoring capabilities with configurable polling intervals.
- Handles file processing workflows by managing file states (processing, completed, failed) within Faspex 5 packages.

### Configuration Instructions

1.  `Add a `**`Faspex5FileProcessing`**` step to your workflow.`
2.  `Configure the Faspex 5 connection settings:`
    - **`Faspex Node`**`: Select the remote node where Faspex server is installed.`
    - **`Faspex Server Address`**`: The full URL or hostname if not using a remote node.`
    - **`Ssh Private key`**`: Private key in PEM format for authentication.`
    - **`Faspex User`**`: Username of the package sender.`
    - **`Faspex API Client ID`**`: Client ID from the Faspex server configuration.`
3.  `Set up the operation parameters:`
    - **`Operation`**`: Choose 'Retrieve the list of files' or 'Update file status'.`
    - **`Max Result`**`: Maximum number of files collected per request.`
    - **`Polling frequency`**`: Interval in seconds for checking files.`
    - **`Continue Monitoring`**`: Enable for continuous monitoring.`

### Example Configuration

| Parameter                 | Example Value                       |
|---------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| **Faspex Node**           | faspex5-server                      |
| **Faspex Server Address** | https://faspex5.company.com         |
| **Ssh Private key**       | -----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY----- ... |
| **Faspex User**           | orchestrator@company.com            |
| **Faspex API Client ID**  | orchestrator-client-12345           |
| **Operation**             | Retrieve the list of files          |
| **Max Result**            | 50                                  |
| **Continue Monitoring**   | checked                             |
| **Polling frequency**     | 30                                  |
| **File Status**           | processing (for update operation)   |
| **Error Message**         | File validation failed              |

### Saved Parameters Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| **Faspex Node** | The node where Aspera Faspex server is installed. If no remote node is configured, use the Advanced parameters section. |
| **Faspex Server Address** | Advanced - The full URL or the host name of the Faspex server, if not configured as Remote Node. |
| **Ssh Private key** | Private key in PEM format for authenticating with the Faspex server. |
| **Faspex User** | The Faspex user for creating the Package. This user will be the sender of the package. |
| **Faspex API Client ID** | The client id provided in the Faspex server Configurations. |
| **Operation** | The operation to execute: 'Retrieve the list of files' to get files to be processed, or 'Update file status' to set the status of a processing file. |
| **Max Result** | The maximum number of collected files for validation by each request. |
| **Continue Monitoring** | If selected, a trigger will be executed every time files are detected for validation. |
| **Polling frequency** | The interval in seconds to check for files to be validated. |
| **File ID** | The id of the file to be validated when the operation selected is 'Update file status'. |
| **File Status** | The file status to set when updating file status. |
| **Error Message** | The custom error message when the file validation fails. |

### Inputs Description

**Mandatory inputs for each operation:**

- **Faspex Server Address**: Faspex5 server address, hostname or remote node name. Required for all operations.
- **Ssh Private Key**: Required to authenticate Orchestrator with Faspex5 server. Must be in PEM format.
- **Faspex API Client ID**: Faspex5 API client id must be provided for authentication.
- **Faspex_sender_user**: Faspex5 user's login username, used as the package sender.

**Mandatory inputs for 'Update file status' operation:**

- **File status**: The file status to set when operation is 'Update file status'.
- **file_id**: The file ID to update when operation is 'Update file status'.

**Optional inputs for 'Retrieve list of files' operation:**

- **max_result**: The maximum number of collected files for validation by each request.
- **polling_frequency**: The interval in seconds to check for files to be validated if 'Continue Monitoring' is selected.

**Optional inputs for 'Update file status' operation:**

- **error_message**: The custom error description if 'file status' value is 'failed'.

### Outputs Description

- **step_information**: Provides a hash with the step details like step_id, step_name, step_status, step_status_details, etc.
- **result_count**: The number of collected files for validation if 'Retrieve list of files' operation is selected.
- **file_transfer_info**: The list of files to be validated if 'Retrieve list of files' operation is selected.

### Supported Operations

- Retrieve the list of files - Get the list of files awaiting processing validation
- Update file status - Set the status of a processing file (processing, completed, failed)

### Operating Instructions

#### Orchestrator Instance Setup

- Generate a new SSH key exclusively for Faspex interaction in PEM format:\
  `ssh-keygen -m pem -b 4096 -C "your_email@example.com"` (save as key_for_faspex.pub)
- Convert to PEM format:\
  `ssh-keygen -f key_for_faspex.pub -m 'PEM' -e > key_for_faspex.pub.pem`
- In HA environments, share the new private key among all Orchestrator instances.
- For Faspex 5.0.4 and later, enable the parameter 'allow_global_key': 'true' or 'Allow signing with global key'.

#### Faspex Server Configuration

**API Client Configuration:**

- Enable JWT grant type: **On**
- Allow refresh token: **Off**
- All users can use this client to access the API
- Public key in PEM format: Paste the SSH public key generated exclusively for Faspex interaction

**User Account Configuration:**

- Log into Faspex 5 with the user account that will be used by Orchestrator
- Navigate to Account settings \> Preferences
- Scroll down to the 'Public key in PEM format (optional)' field
- Paste the same SSH public key generated exclusively for Faspex interaction (same key used in API Client Configuration)

**Package Processing Configuration:**

- Enable File processing: **On**

**Transfer Options Configuration:**

- Disable Downloads during transfers

### Dependencies

- The Faspex5 server must be accessible from the Orchestrator system.
- A valid SSH private key in PEM format is required for authentication.
- The API client must be properly configured in Faspex 5 with JWT grant type enabled.
- File processing must be enabled in the Faspex 5 server configuration.
- The aspweb user must have read permissions for the SSH private key file used for Faspex 5 authentication.

### Troubleshooting

- Authentication errors: Verify that all credentials (Faspex server address, SSH private key, Faspex user, Faspex API client ID) are valid and properly configured.
- Ensure the SSH private key is in the correct PEM format and matches the public key configured in the Faspex API client.
- Check that the Faspex API client has JWT grant type enabled and refresh token disabled.
- Verify that file processing is enabled in the Faspex5 server configuration.
- For Faspex 5.0.4+, confirm that 'allow_global_key' is set to true in the server settings.
- If continuous monitoring is not working, check the polling frequency setting and ensure 'Continue Monitoring' is enabled.
- For file status update failures, verify that the file ID exists and is in a state that allows status updates.

### FASPstream

![FASPstream icon](icons/Faspstream.png){.plugin-icon}

This action plug-in provides the ability to start, monitor and cancel a stream to stream between 2 Aspera transfer servers using faspstream.

| Version | Comment |
|----|----|
| 0.1.0 | Updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8 |
| 0.0.2 | Add @ssh_args = nil and @remote_node = nil to avoid an execution error |
| 0.0.1 | initial release |

#### How It Works

- Enables **high-speed streaming** of live video/audio content using Aspera FASP technology.
- Supports both **sending and receiving** transfer modes between nodes.
- Streams can be **UDP to UDP** (multicast/unicast), **TCP to file**, **file to stream**, or other combinations.
- Monitors stream status via **Node API** with configurable polling frequency.
- Supports stream cancellation via the **workorder or step cancel button**.
- - **Faspstream license** on both initiating and serving nodes
  - **Faspstream hub license** on the receiving side for specific bandwidth
  - **asperanoded and asperacentral services** running on monitored nodes
- Uses **ascp4 binary** for stream execution on the initiating node.

#### Configuration Instructions

1.  Create a new action step using the **Faspstream** plugin.
2.  Fill out the following fields:
    - : The name used to identify a saved Faspstream configured instance.
    - : Comments about this saved Faspstream configured instance.
    - : Remote node name where the stream is initiated from.
    - : Initiating node IP address or domain name.
    - : Initiating node ssh port (33001 by default).
    - : Initiating node ssh private key path.
    - : Initiating node ssh login.
    - : Initiating node ssh password.
    - : Remote node name where the stream is served from.
    - : Serving node IP address or domain name.
    - : Serving node ssh port (33001 by default).
    - : Serving node ssh private key path.
    - : Serving node ssh login.
    - : Serving node ssh password.
    - : A reference to the initiating or serving node for the stream monitoring.
    - : Port to access the monitoring node (9092 by default).
    - : Node api user to access the monitoring node.
    - : Node api password to access the monitoring node.
    - : Stream target rate in Kbps.
    - : Stream minimum rate in Kbps.
    - : Chunk size (64k by default).
    - : Source stream URL.
    - : Destination stream URL.
    - : File path to the ascp4 binary on the initiating node.
    - : Extra ascp4 command line options.
    - : Sending (send) or receiving (recv) mode.
    - : Polling frequency in seconds for the stream monitoring.

#### Example Configuration

This is a minimum setup configuration for the Faspstream plugin.

| Parameter                  | Value                                    |
|----------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| Initiating node            | StreamNode1                              |
| Initiating node ip address | 192.168.1.100                            |
| Initiating node ssh port   | 33001                                    |
| Initiating node username   | aspera                                   |
| Serving node               | StreamNode2                              |
| Serving node ip address    | 192.168.1.200                            |
| Serving node ssh port      | 33001                                    |
| Serving node username      | aspera                                   |
| Node api target            | serving_node                             |
| Node api port              | 9092                                     |
| Target rate                | 10000                                    |
| Minimum rate               | 8000                                     |
| Chunk size                 | 64k                                      |
| Source stream url          | udp://localhost:2001                     |
| Destination stream url     | udp://233.3.3.3:2000?ttl=2&pkt_size=1316 |
| Ascp4 binary path          | /usr/bin/ascp4                           |
| Transfer mode              | send                                     |

#### Saved Parameters Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| **Name** | The name used to identify a saved Faspstream configured instance. |
| **Comments** | Comments about this saved Faspstream configured instance. |
| **Initiating node** | Remote node name where the stream is initiated from. |
| **Initiating node ip address** | Initiating node IP address or domain name. |
| **Initiating node ssh port** | Initiating node ssh port (33001 by default). |
| **Initiating node ssh keypath** | Initiating node ssh private key path. |
| **Initiating node username** | Initiating node ssh login. |
| **Initiating node password** | Initiating node ssh password. |
| **Serving node** | Remote node name where the stream is served from. |
| **Serving node ip address** | Serving node IP address or domain name. |
| **Serving node ssh port** | Serving node ssh port (33001 by default). |
| **Serving node ssh keypath** | Serving node ssh private key path. |
| **Serving node username** | Serving node ssh login. |
| **Serving node password** | Serving node ssh password. |
| **Node api target** | A reference to the initiating or serving node for the stream monitoring. |
| **Node api port** | Port to access the monitoring node (9092 by default). |
| **Node api username** | Node api user to access the monitoring node. |
| **Node api password** | Node api password to access the monitoring node. |
| **Target rate** | Stream target rate in Kbps. |
| **Minimum rate** | Stream minimum rate in Kbps. |
| **Chunk size** | Chunk size (64k by default). |
| **Source stream url** | Source stream URL. |
| **Destination stream url** | Destination stream URL. |
| **Ascp4 binary path** | File path to the ascp4 binary on the initiating node. |
| **Ascp4 command line options** | Extra ascp4 command line options. |
| **Transfer mode** | Sending (send) or receiving (recv) mode. |
| **Monitoring polling frequency** | Polling frequency in seconds for the stream monitoring. |

#### Inputs Description

The list of inputs depends on the configuration of the Faspstream action template. Changes in this list of inputs will override any value set in the action configuration/template.

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| *Initiating node or address* | Remote node name or address/domain name where the stream is initiated from |
| *Initiating node ssh key path* | Initiating node ssh private key path |
| *Initiating node ssh username* | Initiating node ssh login |
| *Initiating node ssh password* | Initiating node ssh password |
| *Initiating node ssh port* | Initiating node ssh port (optional, 33001 by default) |
| *Serving node or address* | Remote node name or address/domain name where the stream is served from |
| *Serving node ssh key path* | Serving node ssh private key path |
| *Serving node ssh username* | Serving node ssh login |
| *Serving node ssh password* | Serving node ssh password |
| *Serving node ssh port* | Serving node ssh port (optional, 33001 by default) |
| *Node API target* | initiating_node or serving_node |
| *Node API port* | Port to access the monitoring node (optional, 9092 by default) |
| *Node API user name* | Node api user to access the monitoring node |
| *Node API user password* | Node api password to access the monitoring node |
| *Target rate* | Stream target rate in Kbps |
| *Minimum rate* | Stream minimum rate in Kbps |
| *Chunk size* | Chunk size (optional, 64k by default) |
| *Source stream URL* | Source stream URL |
| *Destination stream URL* | Destination stream URL |
| *Ascp4 binary path* | File path to the ascp4 binary on the initiating node (optional, default ascp4) |
| *Command line options* | Extra ascp4 command line options (optional) |
| *Transfer mode* | Sending (send) or receiving (recv) mode |

#### Outputs Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| statistics | The statistics as returned by the monitoring API when the session is completed |

#### Supported Actions

None

#### Dependencies

A faspstream license is needed on both initiating and serving sides. The data receiving side (defined by the transfer mode) must have a faspstream hub license defined for a specific bandwidth.

#### Operating Instructions

This plug-in enables high-speed streaming of live video/audio content using Aspera FASP technology.

**Monitoring Configuration\**

- Specify a **node api target** (initiating or serving node) for stream monitoring.
- A node api https request is sent each polling period to the target node to check stream status.
- It can be addressed to either node provided that **asperanoded** and **asperacentral** services are running on them.
- This is always the case on the serving node, not necessarily on the initiating node.
- Specify the node api https port (9092 by default), username and password.
- The node api username can be obtained with the `/opt/aspera/bin/asnodeadmin -l` command on the target node.

**Stream Parameters Configuration\**

- Specify the expected stream **target** and **minimum rate** in Kbps.
- To guarantee good video quality:
  - Minimum rate must be equal to the **video bitrate + packet loss** (about 1 Mbps faster than the encoded rate)
  - Target rate must be **10 to 20% higher** than the video encoded rate
- Specify the **chunk size**. It's the faspstream buffer size used for read/write operations as well as an internal transmission and compression unit (must be at least 4K and limited to 128M).
- Specify the **source** (where to listen from) and **destination** (where to write to) stream URLs.
- URL examples:
  - `udp://localhost:2001` for a unicast UDP
  - `udp://233.3.3.3:2000?ttl=2&pkt_size=1316` for a multicast UDP
  - `tcp://127.0.0.1:5000` for a unicast TCP
  - `file:////opt/work/test/pp3` for a file URL
- Typically a UDP provider is used for stream to stream use cases and a TCP provider is used for stream to file or file to stream use cases.
- On **send** transfer mode, the source URL is on the initiating node and the destination URL is on the serving node.
- On **recv** transfer mode, the source URL is on the serving node and the destination URL is on the initiating node.

**Ascp4 and Transfer Mode Configuration\**

- Specify the **ascp4 binary path** on the initiating node. Examples: `ascp4` or `/usr/bin/ascp4`
- If ascp4 is used instead of the full filepath, make sure that the path to the binary name is included in the OS PATH variable.
- On a **Linux** initiating node, check that the user shell is **bash** rather than aspshell in /etc/passwd.
- On a **Windows** initiating node installed with Aspera Point-to-Point (providing the faspstream ascp4 binary), the delivered OpenSSH software is able to serve the generated command line if the user shell is configured to be **switch.exe** or **sh.exe** (check in the OpenSSH `etc\passwd` file).
- On a **MAC** initiating node, make sure to enable **Sharing + Remote Login** for the addressed user.
- Specify extra command line arguments. See ascp4 man page for a list of them.
- Specify the **transfer mode** (send or recv):
  - On **sending**, the data flows from initiating to serving node
  - On **receiving**, the data flows from serving to initiating node

**Troubleshooting\**

- If you keep seeing the **'Empty faspstream session id'** status message:
  - Make sure that the command line is correct by checking it in the worker logs.
  - Ensure that both nodes have active licenses.
  - Check the node logs for more details (Typically stored at `/var/log/messages`).
  - Verify on the initiating/serving node whether the ascp4 process is started
- The node api target is also used to **cancel a stream** when pressing on the workorder or step cancel button.
- This may be necessary if the stream ascp4 processes (on initiating and serving nodes) must be stopped for any reason.
- **Deleting a workorder** running an active faspstream session will not stop the underlying ascp4 processes.
- Instead, you can **cancel** and then **delete** the workorder.

### FTP transfer

![FTP transfer icon](icons/FtpTransfer.png){.plugin-icon}

This plugin provides a configurable interface to a remote FTP server. It supports sending, retrieving and deleting files within server directories. Username and password authentication are supported when required by the server.

| Version | Comment |
|----|----|
| 1.3.0 | Updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8 |
| 1.2.0 | Enable binary and passive modes by default |
| 1.1.4 | Make upload folder recursively an optional input |
| 1.1.3 | Added log to trace delete after transfers operations |
| 1.1.2 | Fix edge case when ignore invalid source files is selected |
| 1.1.1 | Fix edge case when ignore invalid source files is selected |
| 1.1.0 | Add option to ignore invalid source files |
| 1.0.0 | Add option to control FTP debug level |
| 0.9.4 | Resolved hostname to IP before using ftp component to connect |
| 0.9.3 | Fix for when remote directory does not exist on Filezilla FTP server. |
| 0.9.2 | Fix for when remote directory does not exist on FTP. |
| 0.9.1 | Fix for when mtime command fails on ftp |
| 0.9.0 | Adding Ignore File Listing for speeding up upload. |
| 0.8.2 | Adding Remote Node Dependency |
| 0.8.1 | Change display of progress output |
| 0.8.0 | 2015-08-11 JY - Add option to show true byte count. Progress percentage added. Displays modification time. |
| 0.7.3 | 2015-06-02 ML - Fix validation to actually validate AND to support source_file array. Adds default port |
| 0.7.2 | 2015-02-26 JG - Fixing issues with variable names |
| 0.7.1 | 2015-02-27 ML - New icon |
| 0.7.0 | 2015-02-20 ADM - Add do archive option for uploads |

| 0.5.8 | \- 2014/12/18 - PM -\> Bug fix in inputs spec. A typo from previous commits. local_directory must be optional if already provided |
| 0.5.7 | 2014-12-10 ADM - Support Array as input for FTP UPLOAD ONLY |
| 0.5.6 | Fixed duplicated uploads when using GLOB |
| 0.5.5 | Added protection in case a remote directory does not exist and ftp server returns an unknown error |
| 0.5.4 | Fix to single file upload and improvements to help file |
| 0.5.3 | Prevents autocomplete of user field |
| 0.5.2 | Modified help file to explain the new fields added. |
| 0.5.1 | Folder download processes single file download correctly when download folder flag is checked |
| 0.5.0 | Added functionality to recursively download folders and their contents |
| 0.4.1 | Fixes a bug for single file upload |
| 0.4.0 | Correctly execute the migration |
| 0.3.1 | Adds functionality to recursively upload folders and their contents |
| 0.3.0 | Adds functionality to accept wilcard operators '\*' and '?' in source file names |

| 0.2.2 | Adds display name |
| 0.2.1 | Hides password characters while entering in edit gui. |
| 0.2.0 | Adds passive mode support |

| 0.1.4 | Change reporting to be time-based |
| 0.1.3 | fix bug with self contained remote server |

| 0.1.1 | Removed the infinite timeout and modified parameterized inputs. |
| 0.1.0 | Adds customizable port and node selector |
| 0.0.1 | functional release with no proxy -supports get, put, delete. Use default port |

### How It Works

- Provides a configurable interface to remote FTP servers for file transfer operations including upload (put), download (get), and delete actions.
- Supports both individual file transfers and recursive folder operations with configurable transfer modes (binary/ASCII, active/passive).
- Enables file renaming during transfer, source file removal after successful transfer, and archiving capabilities for uploaded files.
- Includes authentication support with username/password credentials and configurable connection parameters like port and directories.
- Offers performance optimization features such as configurable transfer block sizes and options to ignore file listings before uploads.

### Configuration Instructions

1.  `Add an `**`FTP Transfers`**` step to your workflow.`
2.  `Configure the server connection:`
    - **`Remote Node`**`: Select configured remote system or specify server details manually.`
    - **`Remote Server`**`: FTP server hostname or IP address.`
    - **`Remote Server Port`**`: FTP port (typically 21).`
    - **`Remote User`**`: Username for FTP authentication.`
    - **`Remote Password`**`: Password for FTP authentication.`
3.  `Set up the transfer operation:`
    - **`Action`**`: Choose put (upload), get (download), or delete.`
    - **`Local Directory`**`: Local directory path for file operations.`
    - **`Source file name`**`: Name of file to transfer (supports wildcards).`
    - **`Remote Directory`**`: Target directory on FTP server.`

### Example Configuration

| Parameter                             | Example Value         |
|---------------------------------------|-----------------------|
| **Remote Server**                     | ftp.example.com       |
| **Remote Server Port**                | 21                    |
| **Remote User**                       | ftpuser               |
| **Remote Password**                   | \*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*      |
| **Remote Directory**                  | /uploads              |
| **Action**                            | put                   |
| **Local Directory**                   | /local/files          |
| **Source file name**                  | document.pdf          |
| **Target file name**                  | uploaded_document.pdf |
| **Transfer in binary mode**           | checked               |
| **Transfer in Passive Mode**          | checked               |
| **Remove source file after transfer** | unchecked             |

### Saved Parameters Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| **Remote Node** | Selector for the remote system containing the FTP server. |
| **Remote Server** | Remote server name containing the FTP server, if not using the "Remote node" selector. |
| **Remote Server Port** | The FTP command port normally "21" on the FTP server system. |
| **Remote User** | User name required on the FTP server for authentication. |
| **Remote Password** | FTP server password associated with the user name. Note this is shown in clear text. |
| **Remote Directory** | Directory off of the FTP root directory on the FTP server changed to upon FTP login. |
| **Action** | The FTP action to be performed: "put", "get", or "delete". |
| **Local Directory** | Local directory setting for the client side. |
| **Source file name** | Name of the file being transferred via FTP. |
| **Target file name** | Destination name of file being transferred, if renaming is to be performed. |
| **Transfer in binary mode** | Transfer the file exactly from the source, preserving file byte count and checksum, performing no translation. This is the default. Disable binary mode (enable ASCII mode) only if you need to transfer text files between Linux and Windows and require translation of LF to CR-LF. |
| **Transfer in Passive Mode** | Transfer the file in FTP passive mode, requiring no local port to be open on the sending side. This is the default. Disable passive mode (enable active mode) only if your remote FTP server requires it. |
| **Remove source file after transfer** | Remove the source file upon successful completion of the FTP transfer. |
| **Ignore file listing before upload?** | Check this box if file listing needs to be ignored before the upload operation. This might help speed up the operation. |
| **Upload folder(s) recursively** | Check this box if the source file provided is a folder, and this needs to be uploaded to the FTP server. |
| **Download folder(s) recursively** | Check this box if the remote FTP folder needs to be downloaded to local server. |
| **Transfer block size** | Amount of data copied from application memory space to kernel memory space at one time during FTP transfers. |
| **Archive source file after transfer** | If selected, after a successful FTP upload, the original file is locally moved to an archive folder. |
| **Archive target folder** | If archive after transfer is selected, this field specifies the local target folder for the archive. |
| **Ignore invalid files** | If selected, files that cannot be accessed for upload will be skipped without failing the step. |

### Inputs Description

Changes in this list of inputs will override any values set in the action template.

- **action**: The FTP actions available: "put", "get", or "delete".
- **binary**: Transfer flag, binary or ASCII.
- **local_directory**: Local directory on the client side.
- **remote_directory**: Directory on the FTP server below the default FTP directory.
- **remote_password**: FTP password on the remote server.
- **remote_user**: FTP username on the remote server.
- **remove_after_transfer**: Remove the source file upon successful completion of the transfer.
- **upload_folder**: Uploads a local folder to remote FTP server.
- **allow_download_folder**: Downloads folder from remote FTP server.
- **source_filename**: Source file name.
- **target_filename**: Destination name of the file being transferred if renaming is to be performed.

### Outputs Description

- **size**: The size of the FTP transfer.
- **target_file**: The name of the target file.
- **ignored_files**: The list of files that have been ignored during the upload because not available or not accessible. Only available if 'Ignore invalid files' is selected.

### Operating Instructions

#### Prerequisites

FTP servers require an open port 21; ensure that this port is available on the system containing the Orchestrator instance.

#### Basic Workflow

- Identify the remote FTP server and configure a remote node for it, or place the details of the FTP server in the advanced parameters section. If a particular user name and password is required to access the FTP server it can be placed in either the remote node, or within the advanced parameters settings.
- The FTP server will normally provide a directory that will be the "root" directory for a particular connection. If there are directories that will be below this location, they should be entered in the "remote directory" field.
- If files are going to be delivered to the FTP server, select "put" from the "Action" pull down menu. If files are to be pulled from the FTP server, select "get". To remove a file from the FTP server, select "delete".
- Insert the location that the files on the local system will be uploaded from, or downloaded to in the "Local directory".
- Enter the "Source file name" that is to be acted upon during the transfer. Note that wild card characters - \* and ? - are accepted in source file name.
- When renaming the file at the destination is required, supply the new name in the "Target file name" field.
- If the file being transferred is a binary file, select the "Transfer in binary mode" checkbox.
- If the file being uploaded is a folder, select the "Upload Folder(s) Recursively?" checkbox to upload the folder and its contents.
- If the file being downloaded is a folder, select the "Download Folder(s) Recursively?" checkbox to download the folder and its contents.
- To delete the source file post successful transfer, the "Remove source file after transfer" checkbox must be selected.

### Dependencies

- FTP server must be accessible on the configured port (typically port 21) from the Orchestrator system.
- Valid FTP credentials (username/password) must be available if the FTP server requires authentication.
- The aspweb user must have appropriate local filesystem permissions for source and destination directories.

### Ftps transfer

![Ftps transfer icon](icons/FtpsTransfer.png){.plugin-icon}

This action plugin can be used to perform secure file transfer operations on FTPS servers with SSL/TLS encryption and file renaming support.

| Version | Comment                                             |
|---------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| 0.1.0   | Updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8 |
| 0.0.1   | Initial implementation                              |

#### How It Works

- Provides **secure file transfer operations** to/from remote FTPS (FTP over SSL/TLS) servers.
- Supports **Upload (Put)** and **Download (Get)** operations.
- Uses **SSL/TLS encryption** for secure data transmission over FTP protocol.
- Supports **username/password authentication** for server access.
- Allows **file renaming** during transfer operations.
- Optionally **removes source files** after successful transfer (move operation).
- Connects to FTPS servers via **Remote Node configuration** or direct server credentials.
- Works with configurable **remote directories** relative to the FTPS root.
- Returns **list of files** transferred as output.

#### Configuration Instructions

1.  Create a new action step using the **FtpsTransfer** plugin.
2.  Fill out the following fields:
    - : The name used to identify a saved FTPS Transfer configured instance.
    - : Selector for the remote system containing the FTPS server. Choose from configured Remote Nodes.
    - : Remote server hostname or IP address containing the FTPS server, if not using the 'Remote node' selector.
    - : Username required on the FTPS server for authentication.
    - : FTPS server password associated with the user name.
    - : Directory path relative to the FTPS root directory on the server, changed to upon FTPS login.
    - : The FTPS action to be performed: put (upload) or get (download).
    - : Local directory path on the Orchestrator server for client-side operations.
    - : Name of the file being transferred via FTPS. For put operations, this is the local file; for get operations, this is the remote file.
    - : Destination name of file being transferred, if renaming is to be performed. If blank, original filename is used.
    - : If selected, removes the source file upon successful completion of the FTPS transfer.

#### Example Configuration

This is an example configuration for the FtpsTransfer plugin uploading and renaming a media file.

| Parameter                         | Value                |
|-----------------------------------|----------------------|
| Name                              | Upload Media to FTPS |
| Remote Node                       | ProductionFTPS       |
| Remote Directory                  | /uploads/media       |
| Action                            | put                  |
| Local Directory                   | /local/processed     |
| Source file name                  | video.mp4            |
| Target file name                  | video_final.mp4      |
| Remove source file after transfer | Not selected         |

#### Saved Parameters Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| **Name** | The name used to identify a saved FTPS Transfer configured instance. |
| **Remote Node** | Selector for the remote system containing the FTPS server. Choose from configured Remote Nodes. |
| **Remote Server** | Remote server hostname or IP address containing the FTPS server, if not using the 'Remote node' selector. |
| **Remote User** | Username required on the FTPS server for authentication. |
| **Remote Password** | FTPS server password associated with the user name. |
| **Remote Directory** | Directory path relative to the FTPS root directory on the server, changed to upon FTPS login. |
| **Action** | The FTPS action to be performed: put (upload) or get (download). |
| **Local Directory** | Local directory path on the Orchestrator server for client-side operations. |
| **Source file name** | Name of the file being transferred via FTPS. For put operations, this is the local file; for get operations, this is the remote file. |
| **Target file name** | Destination name of file being transferred, if renaming is to be performed. If blank, original filename is used. |
| **Remove source file after transfer** | If selected, removes the source file upon successful completion of the FTPS transfer. |

#### Inputs Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| *Remote_Node* | The remote node identifier containing the FTPS server configuration. Use this OR Remote_Server (not both). |
| *Remote_Server* | The hostname or IP address of the FTPS server. Use this OR Remote_Node (not both). |
| *Remote_User* | Username for FTPS server authentication (required if server requires authentication). |
| *Remote_Password* | Password for FTPS server authentication (required if server requires authentication). |
| *Remote_Directory* | Path relative to FTPS root directory (e.g., '/incoming/files', 'subfolder/data'). Leave blank for root directory. |
| *Action* | The operation to perform: 'put' (upload) or 'get' (download). |
| *Local_Directory* | Local directory path on Orchestrator server. |
| *Source_file_name* | Name of the file to transfer. Local file for put, remote file for get operations. |
| *Target_file_name* | New name for the file at destination (optional). If blank, uses original source filename. |
| *Remove_source_file_after_transfer* | Boolean flag to delete source file after successful transfer. |

#### Outputs Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| files_list | Array containing the names of the files transferred or listed during the operation |

#### Supported Actions

None

#### Dependencies

None

#### Operating Instructions

This plugin provides a configurable interface to a remote FTPS server. The actions permitted include the ability to send and retrieve files from directory locations exposed by the server. Renaming files during transfer is supported. Username and password authentication is required for server access.

**Supported Actions\**

- **put (Upload):**
  - Transfers files from **local Orchestrator server to remote FTPS server**
  - Source: Local Directory + Source file name
  - Destination: Remote Directory + Target file name (or Source file name if Target is blank)
  - If 'Remove source file after transfer' is selected, local file is deleted after successful upload
  - Example: Upload processed media files to FTPS distribution server
- **get (Download):**
  - Transfers files from **remote FTPS server to local Orchestrator server**
  - Source: Remote Directory + Source file name
  - Destination: Local Directory + Target file name (or Source file name if Target is blank)
  - If 'Remove source file after transfer' is selected, remote file is deleted after successful download
  - Example: Download reports from FTPS server for processing

**Connection Methods\**

- **Using Remote Node (Recommended):**
  - Configure FTPS server details in Remote Node settings in Orchestrator
  - Select the Remote Node in the 'Remote Node' dropdown
  - Server, credentials, and connection settings are managed centrally
  - More secure - credentials stored in Remote Node configuration
  - Reusable across multiple workflow steps
- **Using Direct Server Credentials:**
  - Leave 'Remote Node' blank
  - Specify 'Remote Server' (hostname or IP)
  - Provide 'Remote User' and 'Remote Password' directly
  - Useful for one-off or dynamic server connections

**Remote Directory Concept\**

- The FTPS server typically provides a **root directory** for each connection based on the user account.
- The 'Remote Directory' field specifies a path **relative to this root**.
- **Examples:**
  - FTPS root: `/ftps/user123`
  - Remote Directory: `/incoming/media`
  - Actual server path: `/ftps/user123/incoming/media`
- Leave 'Remote Directory' **blank** to work in the FTPS root directory.
- Use **forward slashes** (/) for directory separators regardless of server OS.

**Common Use Cases\**

- **Media distribution:** Upload processed video or audio files to FTPS servers for delivery.
- **Report retrieval:** Download generated reports from FTPS servers for analysis or distribution.
- **Data exchange:** Secure file exchange with partners or external systems using FTPS.
- **Automated backups:** Upload backup files to remote FTPS storage servers.
- **File cleanup:** Delete processed files from FTPS servers to free up space.
- **File inventory:** List directory contents to discover available files before processing.
- **Archive retrieval:** Download archived files from FTPS servers for restoration or review.

**Best Practices\**

- Use **Remote Node configuration** for credentials rather than direct server parameters for better security.
- Use **FTPS (FTP over SSL/TLS)** instead of plain FTP for encrypted transmission.
- Enable **'Remove source file after transfer'** only when certain the transfer completed successfully.
- Use **absolute paths** for local directories to avoid ambiguity.
- Implement **error handling** in workflows for network failures or authentication issues.
- Monitor the **files_list output** to track which files were processed.
- Test FTPS connectivity and authentication **before deploying workflows** to production.
- Use **Target file name** for renaming when naming conventions differ between systems.
- Ensure FTPS server **firewall rules** allow connections from Orchestrator (typically ports 21 and passive data ports).

**Troubleshooting\**

- **Connection timeout:** Verify FTPS server is reachable, firewall allows connections, and server is running.
- **Authentication failed:** Verify username and password are correct. Check if account is locked or expired.
- **File not found:** Verify file exists in the specified directory on the server.
- **Permission denied:** Ensure FTPS user has appropriate permissions (read for get/list, write for put, delete for remove).
- **Passive mode issues:** FTPS uses passive mode for data connections. Ensure firewall allows passive data port range.
- **SSL/TLS errors:** Verify server certificate is valid or configure to accept self-signed certificates.
- **Directory not found:** Verify 'Remote Directory' path is correct relative to FTPS root.
- **Local file missing:** For put operations, ensure source file exists in Local Directory.
- **Disk space issues:** For get operations, ensure local filesystem has sufficient space.

**Important Notes\**

- FTPS uses **SSL/TLS encryption** for secure file transfers, unlike plain FTP.
- The 'Remote Directory' is **relative to the FTPS root** directory for the authenticated user.
- FTPS typically uses **port 21** for control connection and a range of ports for passive data connections.
- If both 'Remote Node' and 'Remote Server' are specified, **Remote Node takes precedence**.
- The 'Remove source file after transfer' option deletes the source **only after successful transfer**.
- If 'Target file name' is blank, the **original source filename** is used at the destination.
- Ensure FTPS server and network support **passive mode** for data transfers.

**Security Considerations\**

- Store credentials in **Remote Node** configuration rather than direct plugin parameters.
- Use **complex passwords** for FTPS accounts.
- Ensure FTPS server uses valid **SSL/TLS certificates**.
- Grant FTPS users only **necessary permissions** (read-only for downloads, write-only for uploads).
- Configure FTPS server to allow connections only from known Orchestrator IP addresses.
- Enable FTPS **server logging** to track file transfers and access attempts.
- Keep FTPS server software updated with **security patches**.

### FXP Transfer

![FXP Transfer icon](icons/FxpTransfer.png){.plugin-icon}

This action provides the ability to move files using the FXP protocol.

| Version | Comment |
|----|----|
| 0.3.0 | Updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8 |
| 0.2.0 | Added timeout hard coded value of 84600 (24 hours) to read_timeout |
| 0.1.9 | Adding Remote Node Dependency |
| 0.1.8 | add fixes for FTPFXP library to support Filezilla as remote node when no TLS |
| 0.1.7 | add fixes for FTPFXP library to support Filezilla as remote node |
| 0.1.6 | 2015-03-27 ML - New icon |
| 0.1.5 | prevents autocomplete of user field |
| 0.1.4 | after code review |
| 0.1.3 | fix problem when origin_user/password remote_user/password as specified as runtime input |
| 0.1.2 | retrieve node user and password if they are not specified |
| 0.1.1 | fix need to specify the remote file path |
| 0.1.0 | add the possibility to specify the remote file name |
| 0.0.4 | fix problem with remote directory |
| 0.0.3 | fix socket closing when all is ok; use the correct remote_path |
| 0.0.2 | fix problem in setting users and passwords |
| 0.0.1 | functional release |

#### How It Works

- enables direct server-to-server data transfers. Unlike traditional FTP transfers, FXP does not route data through the client’s network connection. The client acts only as a coordinator, while data flows directly between the two servers.
- When an FXP session is initiated, the client establishes and maintains separate FTP control connections to both servers. Using these control connections, the client instructs one server to initiate a direct data transfer with the other server.
- Once the FXP session is established, data flows directly between the servers without passing through the client. This allows efficient transfers even when the client has limited bandwidth.
- To support long-running transfers, a 24-hour timeout is configured in the FTPFXP library. This extended timeout allows large server-to-server transfers to complete without interruption. Transfers exceeding this duration may fail with a timeout error.

#### Configuration Instructions

1.  Create a new action step using the **FxpTransfer** plug-in.
2.  This action transfers data directly between two FTP servers using FXP.
3.  Fill out the following configuration fields:
    - : The remote node used for the FXP transfer.
    - : The remote server IP address. This value overrides the node address if provided.
    - : The remote server port. This value overrides the node port if provided.
    - : The FTP user account on the remote server used for the transfer, if different from the node definition.
    - : The password for the remote FTP user account, if different from the node definition.
    - : Re-enter the remote password to confirm.
    - : The origin node used for the FXP transfer.
    - : The origin server IP address. This value overrides the node address if provided.
    - : The origin server port. This value overrides the node port if provided.
    - : The FTP user account on the origin server used for the transfer, if different from the node definition.
    - : The password for the origin FTP user account, if different from the node definition.
    - : Re-enter the origin password to confirm.
    - : The target directory on the remote server.
    - : The target file name on the remote server.
    - : The handshake protocol to use. Supported values are SSCN (Set Secured Client Negotiation) or the CPSV command.

#### Example Configuration

This is a minimal setup configuration for the FxpTransfer plug-in.

| Parameter          | Value                                 |
|--------------------|---------------------------------------|
| Remote node        | node_2                                |
| Origin node        | node_1                                |
| Remote directory   | /root/src                             |
| Remote file name   | test.txt                              |
| Handshake protocol | SSCN (Set Secured Client Negotiation) |

#### Saved Parameters Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| **Remote node** | The remote node used for the FXP transfer. |
| **Advanced Parameters - Remote Server** | The remote server IP address. This value overrides the node address if provided. |
| **Advanced Parameters - Remote Server port** | The remote server port. This value overrides the node port if provided. |
| **Advanced Parameters - Remote user** | The FTP user account on the remote server used for the transfer, if different from the node definition. |
| **Advanced Parameters - Remote Password** | The password for the remote FTP user account, if different from the node definition. |
| **Advanced Parameters - Confirm Password** | Re-enter the remote password to confirm. |
| **Origin node** | The origin node used for the FXP transfer. |
| **Advanced Parameters - Origin Server** | The origin server IP address. This value overrides the node address if provided. |
| **Advanced Parameters - Origin Server port** | The origin server port. This value overrides the node port if provided. |
| **Advanced Parameters - Origin user** | The FTP user account on the origin server used for the transfer, if different from the node definition. |
| **Advanced Parameters - Origin Server password** | The password for the origin FTP user account, if different from the node definition. |
| **Advanced Parameters - Confirm Password** | Re-enter the origin password to confirm. |
| **Remote directory** | The target directory on the remote server. |
| **Remote file name** | The target file name on the remote server. |
| **Handshake protocol** | The handshake protocol to use. Supported values are SSCN (Set Secured Client Negotiation) or the CPSV command. |

#### Inputs Description

Required inputs depend on how the FxpTransfer template is configured. If dynamic attributes are used, each attribute becomes a runtime input.

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| *Origin_file_path (mandatory)* | The directory path on the origin server used for the transfer. |
| *FXP_debug_flag* | Enable debug logging for the FXP transfer. |
| *Origin_password* | The password for the FTP user account on the origin server. |
| *Origin_server* | The origin server IP address. |
| *Origin_server_port* | The origin server port. |
| *Origin_user* | The FTP user account on the origin server. |
| *Remote_directory* | The target directory on the remote server. |
| *Remote_file_name* | The target file name on the remote server. |
| *Remote_password* | The password for the FTP user account on the remote server. |
| *Remote_server* | The remote server IP address. |
| *Remote_server_port* | The remote server port. |
| *Remote_user* | The FTP user account on the remote server. |
| *Target_file_path* | The full target file path on the remote server. |

#### Outputs Description

None

#### Supported Actions

None

#### Dependencies

None

#### Operating Instructions

1.  FXP enables direct server-to-server file transfers without routing data through the client.
2.  Select and configure the origin and remote FTP servers that will participate in the FXP transfer.
3.  The client establishes separate FTP control connections to both servers to coordinate the transfer.
4.  Once initiated, data flows directly between the origin and remote servers.
5.  Transfer performance depends on the bandwidth available between the two servers rather than the client.
6.  A 24-hour timeout is configured in the FTPFXP library to support large file transfers.
7.  Transfers that exceed the configured timeout may fail with a timeout error.

#### Warnings and Best Practices

- Ensure both FTP servers explicitly allow FXP (server-to-server) transfers, as FXP is often disabled by default for security reasons.
- Verify that firewalls and network security rules allow the required data connections between the two servers.
- Use FXP only between trusted servers to avoid unauthorized access or data exposure.
- For large transfers, ensure sufficient disk space is available on the destination server and monitor transfer progress.

### HTTP Transfer

![HTTP Transfer icon](icons/HttpTransfer.png){.plugin-icon}

This action provides the ability to upload, download and delete files from a remote host using HTTP or HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol or Hypertext Transfer Protocol over SSL).

| Version | Comment |
|----|----|
| 0.2.1 | Add SSL/HTTPS support, redirect following, and response validation |
| 0.2.0 | Updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8 |
| 0.1.4 | Add some elements in the help |
| 0.1.3 | Adding support for HTTP Redirect |
| 0.1.2 | Adding Remote Node Dependency |
