| **Name** | The name used to identify a saved IbmMqMftTransfer configured instance. |
| **Comments** | Comments about this saved IbmMqMftTransfer configured instance. |
| **Execution Node** | Node on which the IBM MQ server is installed. Used when a remote node is configured for MQ. |
| **MQ IP Address** | IP address of the MQ server. Used when no remote Execution Node is configured. |
| **MQ Port** | Port the MQ server listens on. Default is 1414. |
| **Use SSL?** | Check this box to enable SSL/TLS encryption and authentication. |
| **Cipher** | SSL/TLS cipher suite to use (e.g., TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256). Must use the Oracle JRE TLS\_ prefix. |
| **Trust store filename** | Filename of the Java application SSL/TLS trust store (e.g., user.jks). Must be placed in the Orchestrator JRE security directory. |
| **Trust store password** | Password for the trust store file (optional). |
| **MQ Operation Type** | The operation to perform. Currently supported: Submit Transfer. |
| **MQ Channel Name** | Name of the MQ channel to connect through. |
| **MQ Queue Name** | Name of the MQ queue configured with the MFT agent to receive transfer messages. |
| **MQ Queue Manager Name** | Name of the MQ Queue Manager. |
| **MQ Put Request (XML file path)** | Path to the XML file containing the transfer specification. Must be valid XML conforming to the MFT transfer request schema. |
| **MQ Agent Name** | Name of the MFT agent to listen to for transfer status monitoring. |
| **MQ Subscription Queue Name** | Name of the subscription queue used to read transfer status updates. |
| **MQ Subscription Name** | Name of the MQ subscription configured for transfer status monitoring. |
| **MQ Message Id** | Message ID of the submitted transfer, used to retrieve the status of that specific transfer. |
| **MQ User Name** | Username for authenticating with the MQ Queue Manager. |
| **MQ Password** | Password for authenticating with the MQ Queue Manager. |

#### Inputs Description

Runtime inputs can override configured values. Queue Manager Name, Queue Name, Channel Name, Body (file path), and IP address are the core required parameters for submitting a transfer.

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| *Queue Manager Name* | Name of the MQ Queue Manager. Required for all operations. |
| *Queue Name* | Name of the MQ queue configured with the MFT agent. Required for transfer submission. |
| *Channel Name* | Name of the MQ channel to connect through. Required for all operations. |
| *Body* | Path to the XML file containing the transfer specification. Must be valid XML. Required for Submit Transfer operations. |
| *Ip* | IP address of the MQ server. Required when no Execution Node is configured. |
| *Agent Name* | Name of the MFT agent used for transfer status monitoring. |
| *Subscription Name* | Name of the MQ subscription configured for transfer status updates. |
| *Subscription Queue Name* | Name of the queue used to read transfer status messages. |
| *MQ_cipher* | SSL/TLS cipher suite. Required when SSL is enabled. |
| *MQ_trust_store* | Trust store filename. Required when SSL is enabled. |
| *MQ_trust_store_password* | Trust store password. Required when SSL is enabled and the trust store is password-protected. |

#### Outputs Description

The output is the Message ID returned by MQ upon successful transfer submission. Use this ID to track or correlate the transfer in downstream workflow steps.

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| Message_Id | The Message ID returned by MQ upon successful submission of the transfer request. Use this ID to correlate and track the transfer status. |

#### Supported Actions

None

#### Dependencies

Requires IBM MQ to be installed and configured with an MFT agent. Requires Python 3, Flask library, Java Runtime Environment, IBM MQ client libraries, and the MQ Bridge service running on port 8080. The MQ server must be accessible from the Orchestrator host.

#### Operating Instructions

This action submits a file transfer request to IBM MQ MFT by placing an XML payload (read from a file) onto the MFT agent's configured queue, then monitors the transfer status via a subscription queue — all within a single plugin call.

**Transfer XML Payload\**

- The **MQ Put Request** must be a path to a valid XML file conforming to the **MFT FileTransfer.xsd schema**.
- The XML must define the **originator, source agent, destination agent, and transfer set** including source and destination file paths.
- The **Queue Name** must be the queue that the MFT agent is configured to monitor — the agent listens on this queue for incoming transfer requests.
- Transfer submission and status monitoring are performed in a **single plugin call**. Provide both the Queue Name and Subscription Name to enable status tracking.

**Example Transfer XML\**

The following is a sample XML payload for submitting an MFT transfer request (save this to a file and provide the file path):

    <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
    <request version="6.00" xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance"
      xsi:noNamespaceSchemaLocation="FileTransfer.xsd">
      <managedTransfer>
        <originator>
          <hostName>127.0.0.1</hostName>
          <userID>mqm</userID>
        </originator>
        <sourceAgent QMgr="Aspera_Demo" agent="ASPERA_AGENT"/>
        <destinationAgent QMgr="Aspera_Demo" agent="ASPERA_AGENT"
          channel="SYSTEM.DEF.SVRCONN" portNumber="1414"/>
        <transferSet>
          <item checksumMethod="MD5" mode="binary">
            <source disposition="leave" recursive="false" type="file">
              <file>/tmp/myfile.txt</file>
            </source>
            <destination exist="overwrite" type="directory">
              <file>/tmp/archive/</file>
            </destination>
          </item>
        </transferSet>
      </managedTransfer>
    </request>

**MQ Bridge Service Setup\**

- **Important:** If the MQ Bridge service was already set up as part of the **IbmMqIntegration plugin**, do not repeat this setup.
- The plugin requires a **Python-based MQ Bridge service** that wraps the IBM MQ Java SDK JAR file to communicate with IBM MQ servers.
- The MQ Bridge service is located at: `/opt/aspera/orchestrator/script/ibm_mq_plugin_scripts/mq_bridge.py`
- **Prerequisites:**
  - Python 3 must be installed on the system
  - Flask Python library: `pip3 install flask`
  - Java Runtime Environment (JRE) to run the IBM MQ JAR file
  - IBM MQ client libraries installed at `/opt/mqm/bin`
- **Starting the MQ Bridge:**
  - Navigate to the scripts directory: `cd /script/ibm_mq_plugin_scripts`
  - Start the bridge: `./start_mq_bridge.sh`
  - The script will automatically install Flask if not present and start the service in the background
- **Stopping the MQ Bridge:** `./stop_mq_bridge.sh`
- The MQ Bridge listens on **port 8080 by default**. Ensure no other service is using this port before starting.
- Service logs are located at: `/opt/aspera/orchestrator/log/mq_debug.log`
- Health check endpoint: `curl http://localhost:8080/health`
- MQ API message and reason codes reference: [IBM MQ API Codes](https://www.ibm.com/docs/en/ibm-mq/9.4.x?topic=reference-messages-reason-codes)

**SSL/TLS Configuration\**

- The cipher must use the **Oracle JRE `TLS_` prefix** (e.g., `TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256`), not the IBM `SSL_` prefix.
- The cipher must **match the cipher defined on the MQ server side**.
- The trust store file must be placed in: `/lib/java/jre/lib/security/`
- Supported trust store formats: **JKS or PKCS**. Specify the filename including its extension in the Trust store filename field.
- The trust store file must contain the **full chain CA certificate** from the MQ server side.
- Trust store files can be created with tools such as **keytool**, **ikeycmd** (bundled with Java), or **runmqckm** (bundled with IBM MQ).
- The **trust store password is optional**. Enter only if one was defined when the trust store was created.

**Best Practices\**

- Store the **MQ Password and trust store password securely** using Orchestrator's credential management rather than plaintext configuration.
- Validate the **XML transfer specification** against the MFT FileTransfer.xsd schema before using it in a workflow to avoid runtime failures.
- Ensure the **MQ Queue Name matches the queue the MFT agent is monitoring** — using an incorrect queue will result in the transfer never being processed.
- Verify the **MQ Bridge service is running** on port 8080 before executing any MFT workflow steps. Check `mq_debug.log` for startup issues.
- Store the returned **Message ID** output for downstream correlation, auditing, or retry logic.
- For SSL environments, **test the cipher and trust store configuration manually** before integrating into a workflow to isolate connectivity issues.
- Implement **error handling** workflows to manage transfer failures or timeouts when waiting for status updates from the subscription queue.
- If this plugin is used alongside **IbmMqIntegration**, the MQ Bridge service setup only needs to be performed once.
- Ensure the **XML file path is accessible** by the **aspweb** when submitting transfer requests.

**Troubleshooting\**

- **MQ Bridge not running:** Ensure the MQ Bridge service has been started using `./start_mq_bridge.sh` and is listening on port 8080. Check for port conflicts.
- **Flask not installed:** Install Flask using `pip3 install flask` or let the start script install it automatically.
- **Transfer not initiated:** Verify the Queue Name is the queue the MFT agent is configured to monitor. Confirm the MFT agent is running and connected to the Queue Manager.
- **Invalid XML payload:** Ensure the XML file contains well-formed XML and conforms to the MFT FileTransfer.xsd schema. Test the XML independently before use.
- **File not found errors:** Verify the XML file path is correct and accessible from the Orchestrator server.
- **Authentication failure:** Check the MQ User Name and Password. Ensure the user has the required permissions on the Queue Manager.
- **Queue or channel not found:** Verify the Queue Name, Channel Name, and Queue Manager Name are correctly specified and exist on the MQ server.
- **Status not returned:** Confirm the Subscription Name and Subscription Queue Name are correctly configured and the subscription is active on the MQ server.
- **SSL handshake failure:** Confirm the cipher uses the Oracle `TLS_` prefix and matches the MQ server cipher. Verify the trust store file is in the correct directory and contains the MQ server CA certificate.
- **MQ API message and reason codes:** Refer to the [IBM MQ API code references](https://www.ibm.com/docs/en/ibm-mq/9.4.x?topic=reference-messages-reason-codes) for detailed diagnostics.
- **Port 8080 conflict:** If another service is using port 8080, stop it before starting the MQ Bridge, or reconfigure the bridge to use a different port in `mq_bridge.py`.

**Important Notes\**

- The **MQ Put Request Body field now accepts a file path** to an XML file containing the transfer specification, not the XML content itself.
- The **XML file must be valid XML** — the plugin does not accept non-XML payloads for transfer specifications.
- The **Queue Name must be the queue the MFT agent is configured to monitor** for transfer requests, not a general-purpose queue.
- **Transfer submission and status monitoring are a single call** — provide both the Queue Name and Subscription Name to enable end-to-end tracking.
- The **MQ Bridge service must be running** on the Orchestrator host before any MFT workflow step is executed.
- If the MQ Bridge service setup was already completed for the **IbmMqIntegration plugin, do not repeat the setup**.
- SSL cipher values must use the **Oracle JRE `TLS_` prefix** — IBM `SSL_` prefixed values are not supported.
- The trust store file must be placed in the **designated Orchestrator JRE security directory** before the workflow runs.
- Use either **Execution Node or MQ IP Address** to specify the MQ server location — not both simultaneously.

### InfluxDB

![InfluxDB icon](icons/InfluxdbOperation.png){.plugin-icon}

This action plugin provides the ability to interact with InfluxDB

| Version | Comment                                             |
|---------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| 0.1.0   | Updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8 |
| 0.0.3   | Fixes authentication issue                          |
| 0.0.2   | Adding compatibility for Ruby 1.8.7.                |
| 0.0.1   | Initial release.                                    |

### How It Works

- Connects to InfluxDB time-series database to perform query and write operations.
- Supports authentication with username and password for secure database access.
- Executes queries with configurable precision and chunking for optimal performance.
- Writes data points with support for measurements, tags, fields, and timestamps.
- Returns responses in both raw format and structured hash format for flexible data processing.

### Configuration Instructions

1.  `Add an `**`InfluxdbOperation`**` step to your workflow.`
2.  `Configure the database connection parameters:`
    - **`Name`**`: Enter a descriptive name for the InfluxDB operation.`
    - **`Comments`**`: Add notes or description for this step.`
    - **`Name of the database`**`: The name of the database that is to be queried or written to.`
    - **`URL`**`: The URL to the instance of your InfluxDB. Include the port here (e.g., http://localhost:8086).`
    - **`User name`**`: The user name of the account being used to access the database. (Required if authentication is enabled)`
    - **`Password`**`: The password for the account being used to access the database. (Required if authentication is enabled)`
3.  `Select the operation type:`
    - **`Operation`**`: Choose between Query or Write operation.`
4.  `For `**`Query`**` operations, configure:`
    - **`Query`**`: The query to be executed on the database. Multiple commands can be delimited with semicolons.`
    - **`Chunk size`**`: Optional integer that determines how InfluxDB will chunk its responses. If left blank, responses will not be chunked.`
    - **`Precision`**`: Determines with how much precision the epoch timestamps will be returned (e.g., ns, u, ms, s, m, h).`
5.  `For `**`Write`**` operations, configure:`
    - **`Data`**`: The data to be written to the database. Format: <measurement>,<tag_key>=<tag_value> <field_key>=<field_value> <timestamp>`
    - **`Consistency`**`: Determines the write consistency for the point. Only used for InfluxDB Enterprise clusters.`
    - **`Retention Policy`**`: Optional parameter that sets the target retention policy for the write. If blank, InfluxDB writes to the DEFAULT retention policy.`
    - **`Precision`**`: Determines the precision for the supplied Unix time values (e.g., ns, u, ms, s, m, h).`

### Example Configuration - Query Operation

| Parameter                | Example Value                             |
|--------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Name**                 | Get Temperature Metrics                   |
| **Comments**             | Retrieves Temperature metrics from the DB |
| **Name of the database** | system_metrics                            |
| **URL**                  | http://influxdb-server:8086               |
| **User name**            | admin                                     |
| **Password**             | \*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*                          |
| **Operation**            | Query                                     |
| **Query**                | SELECT \* FROM temperature                |
| **Chunk size**           | 10000                                     |
| **Precision**            | ms                                        |

### Example Configuration - Write Operation

| Parameter                | Example Value                          |
|--------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Name**                 | Write Temperature Data                 |
| **Comments**             | Writes temperature readings to the DB  |
| **Name of the database** | system_metrics                         |
| **URL**                  | http://influxdb-server:8086            |
| **User name**            | admin                                  |
| **Password**             | \*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*                       |
| **Operation**            | Write                                  |
| **Data**                 | temperature,location=office value=25.3 |
| **Consistency**          | one                                    |
| **Retention Policy**     | Blank                                  |
| **Precision**            | ns                                     |

### Saved Parameters Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| **Name** | Display name for this InfluxDB operation instance. |
| **Comments** | Notes or description for this step. |
| **Name of the database** | The name of the database that is to be queried or written to. |
| **URL** | The URL to the instance of your InfluxDB. Include the port here (e.g., http://localhost:8086). |
| **User name** | The user name of the account being used to access the database. (Required if authentication is enabled) |
| **Password** | The password for the account being used to access the database. (Required if authentication is enabled) |
| **Operation** | The operation to be performed. Available operations are Query and Write. |
| **Query** | (Query only) The query to be executed on the database. Multiple commands can be delimited with semicolons. |
| **Chunk size** | (Query only) Integer that determines how InfluxDB will chunk its responses. Optional - if left blank, responses will not be chunked. |
| **Precision** | (Query/Write) Determines the precision for epoch timestamps (Query) or Unix time values (Write). Values: ns, u, ms, s, m, h. |
| **Data** | (Write only) The data to be written to the database. Format: \<measurement\>,\<tag_key\>=\<tag_value\> \<field_key\>=\<field_value\> \<timestamp\>. Multiple points separated by newlines. |
| **Consistency** | (Write only) Determines the write consistency for the point. Only used for InfluxDB Enterprise clusters. |
| **Retention Policy** | (Write only) Optional parameter that sets the target retention policy for the write. If blank, writes to DEFAULT retention policy. |

### Inputs Description

Changes in this list of inputs will override any values set in the action template.

- **database_name**: The name of the database to query or write to.
- **url**: The URL to the InfluxDB instance including port.
- **username**: Username for database authentication.
- **password**: Password for database authentication.
- **operation**: The operation to perform (Query or Write).
- **query**: (Query only) The query to execute on the database.
- **chunk_size**: (Query only) How InfluxDB will chunk responses.
- **data**: (Write only) The data points to write to the database.
- **consistency**: (Write only) Write consistency level for Enterprise clusters.
- **retention_policy**: (Write only) Target retention policy for the write.
- **precision**: Timestamp precision (ns, u, ms, s, m, h).

### Outputs Description

- **Response_Code**: The HTTP status code received from InfluxDB. 200 = successful query, 204 = successful write, 400 = bad request (syntax error), 401 = authorization failed (invalid credentials).
- **Raw_Output**: (Query only) The raw output as returned by InfluxDB.
- **Hash_Output**: (Query only) The response from InfluxDB in a hash/structured format.

### Supported Actions

None

### Dependencies

- Access to an InfluxDB instance (version 1.x) with network connectivity.
- Valid database credentials if authentication is enabled on the InfluxDB instance.
- Appropriate user permissions (read access for queries, write access for writes).

### Operating Instructions

#### Overview

The InfluxdbOperation plugin provides integration with InfluxDB time-series databases, allowing workflows to query data and write data points. The plugin supports both authenticated and non-authenticated connections and provides flexible options for data precision and chunking.

#### Database Connection Setup

- **URL format**: Include the protocol and port in the URL (e.g., http://influxdb-server:8086 or https://influxdb-server:8086 for SSL).
- **Authentication**: If your InfluxDB instance has authentication enabled, provide both username and password. These parameters are optional for instances without authentication.
- **Database name**: Specify the exact name of the database you want to query or write to. The database must exist before performing operations.
- **User permissions**: Ensure the user account has appropriate permissions - read access for queries, write access for writes.

#### Query Operations

\

##### Query Syntax

- Use InfluxQL syntax for queries (SELECT statements, WHERE clauses, GROUP BY, etc.).
- Multiple queries can be executed in a single operation by separating them with semicolons.
- Common query patterns: `SELECT * FROM "measurement" WHERE time > now() - 1h`
- Use aggregation functions: `SELECT mean("field") FROM "measurement" GROUP BY time(5m)`

\

##### Chunking and Precision

- **Chunk size**: Controls how InfluxDB streams large result sets. Specify an integer to chunk by number of points, or leave blank for no chunking. Useful for large datasets to manage memory usage.
- **Precision options**: Controls timestamp format in query results:
- \- `ns`: Nanoseconds (default)
- \- `u`: Microseconds
- \- `ms`: Milliseconds
- \- `s`: Seconds
- \- `m`: Minutes
- \- `h`: Hours

#### Write Operations

\

##### Data Format

Data must be formatted in InfluxDB line protocol:

`<measurement>[,<tag_key>=<tag_value>...] <field_key>=<field_value>[,<field_key>=<field_value>...] [<timestamp>]`

- **Measurement** (required): The name of the measurement (like a table name).
- **Tags** (optional): Key-value pairs for indexed metadata. Format: `tag1=value1,tag2=value2`
- **Fields** (required): At least one field key-value pair. Format: `field1=value1,field2=value2`
- **Timestamp** (optional): Unix timestamp. If omitted, InfluxDB uses server time. Precision specified by the Precision parameter.
- **Multiple points**: Separate each point with a newline character to write multiple data points in a single request.

\

##### Write Examples

- Basic write: `temperature,location=room1 value=22.5`
- With timestamp: `temperature,location=room1 value=22.5 1609459200000000000`
- Multiple tags and fields: `cpu,host=server01,region=us-west usage_idle=20,usage_system=10 1609459200`
- Multiple points (separated by newline):\
  `temperature,location=room1 value=22.5`\
  `temperature,location=room2 value=23.1`

\

##### Write Options

- **Retention Policy**: Determines how long InfluxDB keeps the data. If blank, InfluxDB uses the DEFAULT retention policy.
- **Consistency**: Only applies to InfluxDB Enterprise clusters. Options: one, quorum, all, any. Determines how many data nodes must confirm the write.
- **Precision**: Specifies the format of the timestamp in your data. Must match the precision of your supplied timestamps.

#### Response Codes

- **200 OK**: Query operation completed successfully.
- **204 No Content**: Write operation completed successfully.
- **400 Bad Request**: Syntax error in query or malformed data in write operation. Check the query/data format.
- **401 Unauthorized**: Authentication failed. Verify username and password are correct.
- **404 Not Found**: Database does not exist. Verify the database name.
- **500 Internal Server Error**: InfluxDB encountered an error. Check InfluxDB logs for details.

#### Output Processing

- **Response_Code**: Always check this output first to verify the operation succeeded (200 for queries, 204 for writes).
- **Raw_Output**: Contains the complete JSON response from InfluxDB for queries. Use this when you need the exact response format.
- **Hash_Output**: Provides a structured Ruby hash format of the query response, making it easier to parse and process results in subsequent workflow steps.
- **Error handling**: For non-success response codes, the raw output will contain error details from InfluxDB.

#### Best Practices

- Always validate Response_Code output before processing query results or assuming write success.
- Use appropriate precision settings to balance between timestamp accuracy and performance.
- For large queries, implement chunking to manage memory usage and improve response times.
- Include error handling in your workflows to manage authentication failures and syntax errors.
- Use tags for indexed metadata that you'll filter on, and fields for actual measured values.
- When writing multiple points, batch them in a single write operation for better performance.
- Test queries and data formats using the InfluxDB CLI or web interface before implementing in workflows.
- Consider setting up retention policies appropriate for your data storage requirements.
- For production environments, use authentication and secure connections (HTTPS).
- Monitor the InfluxDB instance performance when implementing high-frequency write operations.

### JIRA Ticket Creation

![JIRA Ticket Creation icon](icons/JiraOperation.png){.plugin-icon}

This action plug-in provides the ability to create JIRA tickets.

| Version | Comment                                             |
|---------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| 0.2.0   | Updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8 |
| 0.1.0   | Add component and ssl vefification                  |
| 0.0.2   | Changing plugin name.                               |
| 0.0.1   | Initial release.                                    |

- ### Description

  #### Saved Parameters Description

- **Name**: The name used to identify a saved JiraCreate configured instance.

- **Comments**: Some comments about this saved JiraCreate configured instance.

- **Title**: Title of the ticket to be created.

- **Description**: Description of the issue to be created

- **Jira url**: Your company's JIRA url.

- **Username**: Your JIRA username.

- **Password**: Your JIRA password.

- **Ssl verification**: To verify the validity of the SSL certificate presented by the Jira server.

- **Ticket Type**: Type of ticket describing the issue.

- **Priority**: Priority of the issue being created.

- **Assignee**: This parameter is optional. If left blank the default assignee will be used.

- **Project key**: Project key that the issue should be filed under.

- **Component**: Component name. Optional.

##### Input description

The list of inputs depends on the configuration of the JiraCreate action template.

##### Outputs description

- **Ticket_Id**: ID of the created ticket.
- **Create_Ticket_Response**: Response of the ticket creation such as: { "id" =\> "275075", "key" =\> "VODT-3688", "self" =\> "https://jira.xxx.net/rest/api/2/issue/275075" }
- **Ticket_JSON_Info**: URL of the created ticket such as: https://jira.xxx.net/rest/api/2/issue/275075
- **Error_Message**: Error message returned by the Jira server

##### Operating Instructions

The JIRA URL must be like https://jira.xxx.net\
Don't add a forward slash at the end of the URL.\
HTTP is not supported.\
\
Caution: you must enter the project key (e.g. VODT), not the project id (e.g. 11852).\
'400 Bad Request' is returned if the project key value is unknown.\
'400 Bad Request' is returned if the component is required by the Jira server and the value is unknown.\
\
The ticket type and priority can be set in input of the plugin to enter a custom value (e.g. type 'Task' instead of the pre-defined 'Testing Task').

### Kafka stream

![Kafka stream icon](icons/KafkaStream.png){.plugin-icon}

This plugin is used to send/receive messages to/from an Apache Kafka system.

| Version | Comment                                                    |
|---------|------------------------------------------------------------|
| 0.1.2   | Updated the label from Keep ongoing to Continue Monitoring |
| 0.1.1   | Updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8        |
| 0.1.0   | Remove hardcoded aspera directory reference                |
| 0.0.2   | Update path to ruby                                        |
| 0.0.1   | Initial implementation                                     |

#### How It Works

- Provides integration with **Apache Kafka** for message streaming operations.
- Supports **PUT (write/produce)** and **GET (read/consume)** operations.
- Enables **publishing messages to Kafka topics** from Orchestrator workflows.
- Enables **consuming messages from Kafka topics** to trigger or feed workflow processing.
- Supports **pattern matching** with regular expressions to filter consumed messages.
- Can operate in **trigger mode** to continuously monitor topics for new messages.
- Supports **consumer groups** for parallel message processing across multiple consumers.
- Provides **partition control** for targeted message placement and retrieval.
- Supports **message keys** for log compaction and partition assignment.
- Optional **SSL/TLS encryption** for secure message transmission.
- Returns message body, key, offset, and partition information.

#### Configuration Instructions

1.  Create a new action step using the **KafkaStream** plugin.
2.  Fill out the following fields:
    - : Name of the Action plugin in the workflow.
    - : Comments given to the step.
    - : Remote Node addressing the Kafka system. Specify the Kafka port in the TCP port field, not in the SSH port field, within the Remote node configuration.
    - : If no selected remote node, enter here the Kafka system IP address or domain name.
    - : If no selected remote node, enter the Kafka system port. Default is 9092.
    - : PUT (write to) and GET (read from) operations are supported.
    - : Topic to subscribe to. Can be a comma-separated list of topics.
    - : Consumer group identifier. GET operation only. Used for parallel processing and checkpointing.
    - : Regular expression for pattern matching on the message body. GET operation only.
    - : Read from the first message in the topic instead of reading only new messages. GET operation only. Applies only for new group IDs.
    - : Return all matching messages as a list. If not selected, return a single matching message. GET operation only.
    - : Keep triggering on new messages for continuous monitoring. GET operation only.
    - : Polling period in seconds. Default is 10 seconds. GET operation only.
    - : Partition number for targeted read/write operations.
    - : Partition key ensuring messages with the same key go to the same partition. PUT operation only.
    - : Message key stored alongside message value. Used for log compaction and state restoration.
    - : Set synchronous (blocking) or asynchronous (non-blocking) producer. PUT operation only.
    - : The message payload to send. PUT operation only.
    - : Enable SSL/TLS message encryption.
    - : File path on Orchestrator of your SSL/TLS certificate. Must be in X509 format (e.g., ca_cert.pem), not JKS format.

#### Example Configuration

This is an example configuration for the KafkaStream plugin consuming messages from Kafka topics.

| Parameter            | Value                  |
|----------------------|------------------------|
| Name                 | Consume Order Messages |
| Execution node       | KafkaCluster01         |
| Operation Type       | GET                    |
| Topic                | orders, returns        |
| Group id             | orchestrator_processor |
| Pattern matching     | "status":"pending"     |
| Read from beginning? | Not selected           |
| Return all?          | Selected               |
| Continue Monitoring  | Selected               |
| Polling frequency    | 10                     |

#### Saved Parameters Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| **Name** | Name of the Action plugin in the workflow. |
| **Comments** | Comments given to the step. |
| **Execution node** | Remote Node addressing the Kafka system. Specify the Kafka port in the TCP port field, not in the SSH port field, within the Remote node configuration. |
| **Remote address** | If no selected remote node, enter here the Kafka system IP address or domain name. |
| **Remote port** | If no selected remote node, enter the Kafka system port. Default is 9092. |
| **Operation Type** | PUT (write to) and GET (read from) operations are supported. |
| **Topic** | Topic to subscribe to. Can be a comma-separated list of topics. |
| **Group id** | Consumer group identifier. GET operation only. Used for parallel processing and checkpointing. |
| **Pattern matching** | Regular expression for pattern matching on the message body. GET operation only. |
| **Read from beginning?** | Read from the first message in the topic instead of reading only new messages. GET operation only. Applies only for new group IDs. |
| **Return all?** | Return all matching messages as a list. If not selected, return a single matching message. GET operation only. |
| **Continue Monitoring** | Keep triggering on new messages for continuous monitoring. GET operation only. |
| **Polling frequency** | Polling period in seconds. Default is 10 seconds. GET operation only. |
| **Partition id** | Partition number for targeted read/write operations. |
| **Partition key** | Partition key ensuring messages with the same key go to the same partition. PUT operation only. |
| **Message key** | Message key stored alongside message value. Used for log compaction and state restoration. |
| **Sync producer?** | Set synchronous (blocking) or asynchronous (non-blocking) producer. PUT operation only. |
| **Message body** | The message payload to send. PUT operation only. |
| **Use SSL?** | Enable SSL/TLS message encryption. |
| **Certificate filepath** | File path on Orchestrator of your SSL/TLS certificate. Must be in X509 format (e.g., ca_cert.pem), not JKS format. |

#### Inputs Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| *Remote_address* | Kafka remote system IP address or domain name (e.g., kafka.example.com or 192.168.1.100). |
| *Remote_port* | Kafka remote system port. Default is 9092. |
| *Topic* | Topic to subscribe to. Can be a comma-separated list (e.g., 'topic1, topic2, topic3'). |
| *Group_id* | Consumer group identifier. GET operation only. Required for GET operations. |
| *Pattern* | Regular expression for message body pattern matching. GET operation only. Optional. |
| *Message_body* | Message payload to send. PUT operation only. Required for PUT operations. |
| *Partition_id* | Partition number for targeted operations. Optional. |
| *Partition_key* | Partition key for consistent partition assignment. PUT operation only. Optional. |
| *Message_key* | Message key stored with message value. Optional. |
| *Certificate_path* | File path on Orchestrator of SSL/TLS certificate in X509 format. Required if Use SSL? is selected. |

#### Outputs Description

If 'Return all?' is selected, each of the parameters below will be an array, with one element per matching message.

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| Message_Body | The sent or received message body (payload). If 'Return all?' is selected, returns an array of message bodies. |
| Message_Key | The received message key. GET operation only. If 'Return all?' is selected, returns an array of message keys. |
| Message_Offset | The received message offset within the partition. GET operation only. If 'Return all?' is selected, returns an array of offsets. |
| Partition_ID | The received message partition ID. GET operation only. If 'Return all?' is selected, returns an array of partition IDs. |

#### Supported Actions

None

#### Dependencies

None

#### Operating Instructions

This plugin provides integration with Apache Kafka for message streaming. Both PUT (produce) and GET (consume) operations are supported.

**PUT Operation (Produce Messages)\**

- **Purpose:** Produce/write messages to Kafka topics.
- **Topics:**
  - Specify one or more topics as comma-separated list
  - Example: `orders, notifications, audit_log`
  - Spaces before/after topic names are automatically removed
- **Message Body:** The payload to send. Can be plain text, JSON, XML, or any string format.
- **Partition ID:**
  - Optional: Write message to a specific partition identified by numeric ID
  - If not specified, Kafka uses partition key or default partitioner
- **Partition Key:**
  - Optional: Alphanumeric key ensuring messages with same key go to same partition
  - Useful for maintaining message order for related events
  - Example: Use customer_id as partition key to keep all customer messages in same partition
- **Message Key:**
  - Optional: Stored alongside message value
  - Used for log compaction to restore state after crashes
  - Different from partition key - partition key determines partition, message key is metadata
- **Synchronous vs Asynchronous:**
  - **Synchronous (Sync producer? is selected):** Blocking - waits for message delivery confirmation. Guarantees delivery but slower.
  - **Asynchronous (Sync producer? is not selected):** Non-blocking - returns immediately. Better performance but no delivery guarantee.

**GET Operation (Consume Messages)\**

- **Purpose:** Consume/read messages from Kafka topics.
- **Topics:** Specify one or more topics as comma-separated list.
- **Group ID (Required):**
  - Used for consumer parallelism and checkpointing
  - Messages published to a topic are delivered to **only one consumer instance** within a subscribing consumer group
  - Consumer instances in **different groups** receive the **same messages** if subscribed to same topic
  - Example: Use group_id='processor_A' and group_id='processor_B' to have both process all messages
- **Partition ID:**
  - Optional: Return only messages from specific partition
  - Useful for targeted consumption from known partition
- **Message Key:**
  - Optional: Return only messages with this specific key
  - Filters messages based on message key metadata
- **Pattern Matching:**
  - Optional: Regular expression matched against message body
  - Returns only messages where body matches the pattern
  - Example: `"status":"pending"` matches JSON messages with pending status
- **Read from Beginning:**
  - Returns all messages in topic starting from first message
  - **Important:** Only applies when starting plugin with a **new group ID**
  - Kafka checkpointing preserves position - subsequent runs return only new messages
  - Checkpointing enables resuming after consumer crashes
- **Return All:**
  - If selected: Returns all matching messages as arrays
  - If not selected: Returns single matching message
  - When enabled, outputs become arrays (Message_Body\[\], Message_Key\[\], etc.)
- **Continue Monitoring:**
  - Continuously polls for new messages at specified polling frequency
  - Creates new workflow instance for each batch of matching messages
  - **WARNING:** Do not run 2 instances with same topic + group simultaneously

**Consumer Groups and Checkpointing\**

- **Purpose:** Enable parallel message processing and fault tolerance.
- **Single Group, Multiple Consumers:**
  - Messages distributed across consumers in the same group
  - Each message delivered to **only one** consumer instance
  - Enables parallel processing of high-volume topics
  - Example: 3 consumers in group 'processors' share message load
- **Multiple Groups:**
  - Each group receives **all messages** from subscribed topics
  - Enables multiple independent processing pipelines
  - Example: group 'analytics' and group 'storage' both receive all messages
- **Checkpointing:**
  - Kafka tracks each consumer's position (offset) within each partition
  - Enables resuming from last processed message after crash/restart
  - Automatic - no manual intervention required
  - Persists across consumer restarts with same group ID

**Important Limitations\**

- **Maximum message size:** 1MB by default (configurable on Kafka cluster).
- **Message deletion:** Controlled by Kafka based on retention policy (age or size), not by consumer reads.
- **Concurrent triggers warning:** Do NOT run 2 'Continue Monitoring' instances with same topic + group simultaneously.
- **Certificate format:** Must use X509 format (e.g., .pem), not JKS format.

**Common Use Cases\**

- **Event-driven workflows:** Trigger workflows when events published to Kafka topics (orders, notifications, alerts).
- **Data pipeline integration:** Consume messages from data streams for processing, transformation, or storage.
- **Microservices communication:** Publish workflow results to Kafka for other services to consume.
- **Audit logging:** Write workflow events and actions to Kafka audit topics.
- **Real-time analytics:** Feed Kafka messages into analytics workflows for real-time processing.
- **Multi-system coordination:** Use Kafka as message bus for coordinating across multiple systems.
- **Retry/DLQ patterns:** Publish failed processing results to dead-letter queue topics.

**Best Practices\**

- Use **consumer groups** for parallel processing and fault tolerance.
- Set **unique group IDs** for different processing pipelines to ensure all receive messages.
- Use **partition keys** when message order matters for related events.
- Use **synchronous producers** for critical messages requiring delivery guarantees.
- Use **asynchronous producers** for high-throughput scenarios where occasional loss is acceptable.
- Set appropriate **polling frequency** - balance between latency and overhead.
- Avoid running **multiple 'Continue Monitoring' instances** with same topic + group.
- Keep messages **under 1MB** - use external storage for large payloads and reference in message.
- Use **pattern matching** to filter messages and reduce processing overhead.
- Monitor **consumer lag** on Kafka cluster to ensure workflows keep up with message volume.

**Troubleshooting\**

- **Connection timeout:** Verify Kafka broker is reachable, port 9092 is accessible, and firewall allows connections.
- **Authentication failed:** Verify Kafka cluster authentication settings match plugin configuration.
- **Topic not found:** Verify topic exists on Kafka cluster and has been created.
- **No messages consumed:** Check if messages exist in topic, group ID is correct, and offsets haven't passed available messages.
- **'Timed out while waiting for response' error:** Multiple 'Continue Monitoring' instances with same topic + group running. Stop all instances and restart one with different group ID.
- **SSL connection failure:** Verify certificate path is correct and certificate is in X509 format.
- **Message too large:** Reduce message size below 1MB or increase Kafka cluster's max message size configuration.
- **Pattern not matching:** Test regex pattern separately, remember pattern matches against message body only.

**Important Notes\**

- The Kafka **port must be specified in TCP port field**, not SSH port field, in Remote Node configuration.
- Default Kafka port is **9092** (broker port), not 2181 (Zookeeper port).
- **Topics can be comma-separated lists**. Spaces before/after names are automatically removed.
- 'Read from beginning' only applies for **new group IDs**. Existing groups resume from checkpointed offset.
- When 'Return all?' is enabled, **all outputs become arrays** (Message_Body\[\], Message_Key\[\], etc.).
- **Do not run multiple 'Continue Monitoring'** instances with same topic + group simultaneously.
- Messages are **not deleted after consumption**. Deletion controlled by Kafka retention policy.
- Maximum message size is typically **1MB** (configurable on Kafka cluster).
- **Partition key** determines partition assignment. **Message key** is separate metadata for log compaction.
- **Synchronous producers** guarantee delivery but have lower throughput than asynchronous.
- SSL/TLS requires X509 certificate format (.pem), **not JKS format**.

### Local execution

![Local execution icon](icons/LocalExecution.png){.plugin-icon}

This action plug-in provides the ability to invoke a third-party application or a script running locally on the workflow server. The integration mechanism conforms to the CGI-BIN protocol used by web and application servers (i.e. input parameters are provided as environment variable, output is provided on the stdio).

| Version | Comment |
|----|----|
| 0.4.0 | Removing Windows support code, Updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8 |
| 0.3.4 | Removed explicit html escaping since not needed with new Ruby |
| 0.3.3 | Added styling to parameter help text. |
| 0.3.2 | Allow user to specify their own status code when error/failure occurs |
| 0.3.1 | Display output from progress read at the end of process execution |
| 0.3.0 | Add escaping for single quotes |
| 0.2.9 | Fix UI glitch preventing display of empty commands |
| 0.2.8 | Restores compatibility with version of Orchestrator pre 2.2.1 |
| 0.2.7 | New icon, fixes executor script in Linux to support parameters with quote, fixes configurator to allow quotes |
| 0.2.6 | Changes the execution to be made from the /opt/aspera/scripts (EXECUTABLE_DIRECTORY) |
| 0.2.5 | Bad - upgrade to 0.2.6 |
| 0.2.4 | Fixes mis-matched between LOAD and ROOT of actions |
| 0.2.3 | Minor clean-up with no functionality changes |
| 0.2.2 | Moves script directory to /avoid install over-write/opt/aspera/scripts to avoid upgrade overwrite |
| 0.2.1 | Adds clean-up of temporary files from var run |
| 0.2.0 | Adds parallel execution mode, configurable outputs. DB_stored_scripts field was added to DB but is unused. |

#### How It Works

- This action plug-in executes a third-party application or script locally on the workflow server.
- The integration mechanism follows a CGI-style protocol commonly used by web and application servers, where input parameters are provided as environment variables and output is returned via standard input/output (stdio).

#### Configuration Instructions

1.  Create a new action step using the **LocalExecution** plug-in.
2.  This action executes a local script or executable and captures its outputs.
3.  Fill out the following configuration fields:
    - : The name used to identify a saved LocalExecution configuration.
    - : Optional comments about this LocalExecution configuration.
    - : The command line to execute. This string may include variables that are replaced with their runtime values during execution. The command is constructed by appending the selected executable to the command arguments defined in the configuration interface. The executable must be located in the /opt/aspera/scripts directory.
    - : When selected, the standard output (stdio) is returned as an array in the Multi output variable instead of a single string in the stdio output variable.
    - : Defines required inputs that are passed to the script or executable as environment variables.
    - : Defines optional inputs that are passed to the script or executable as environment variables.
    - : When selected, the script or executable can report intermediate status information while it is running. The status reporting API is described in the Operating Instructions section.
    - : Defines the list of typed outputs expected from the script or executable. The output extraction API is described in the Operating Instructions section.

#### Example Configuration

This is a minimal setup configuration for the LocalExecution plug-in.

| Parameter          | Value                      |
|--------------------|----------------------------|
| Command            | ./process_files.sh         |
| Multiline          | Selected                   |
| Mandatory Inputs   | file_path, destination_dir |
| Optional Inputs    | log_level                  |
| Parallel Execution | Selected                   |
| Formatted Outputs  | Result, StatusMessage      |

#### Saved Parameters Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| **Name** | The name used to identify a saved LocalExecution configuration. |
| **Comments** | Optional comments about this LocalExecution configuration. |
| **Command** | The command line to execute. This string may include variables that are replaced with their runtime values during execution. The command is constructed by appending the selected executable to the command arguments defined in the configuration interface. The executable must be located in the /opt/aspera/scripts directory. |
| **Multiline** | When selected, the standard output (stdio) is returned as an array in the Multi output variable instead of a single string in the stdio output variable. |
| **Mandatory Inputs** | Defines required inputs that are passed to the script or executable as environment variables. |
| **Optional Inputs** | Defines optional inputs that are passed to the script or executable as environment variables. |
| **Parallel Execution** | When selected, the script or executable can report intermediate status information while it is running. The status reporting API is described in the Operating Instructions section. |
| **Formatted Outputs** | Defines the list of typed outputs expected from the script or executable. The output extraction API is described in the Operating Instructions section. |

#### Inputs Description

Required inputs depend on how the LocalExecution template is configured. If dynamic attributes are used, each attribute becomes a runtime input.

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| *Command line inputs (mandatory)* | All variables referenced in the command line are mandatory and must be of type String. |
| *Environment variable inputs* | Variables listed in the Mandatory or Optional Inputs fields may be typed, but are converted to strings before being passed as environment variables. |

#### Outputs Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| Multi | Returned only when Multiline is selected. Contains stdio output split into an array, with one entry per line. |
| stdio | Returned only when Multiline is not selected. Contains the full stdio output as a single string. |
| Formatted Outputs | If configured, contains the values extracted from stdio based on the definitions in the Formatted Outputs configuration field. |

#### Supported Actions

None

#### Dependencies

None

#### Operating Instructions

1.  This action executes the configured command line. The executable or script receives some inputs as command-line arguments and others as environment variables. The command-line string may include static text as well as variables that are expanded at runtime. Variables are indicated using the syntax \<%=variable_name%\> embedded within the command string.\
    For example: "file_scan.sh \<%=file_name%\> -s \<%=icap_server_name%\>". The executable or script must be located in `/tmp`, be accessible to the `aspweb` user, and have the appropriate permissions to execute. Additionally, scripts must include a valid shebang line.
2.  When Parallel Execution is enabled, the executable can report status updates using the following format:\
    '-#STATUS--#--STATUS MESSAGE#-'\
    Either STATUS or STATUS MESSAGE may be omitted.\
    For example: '-#Error--#--Action was stopped#-' updates both the status and message, while '-#--#--File tester.txt was scanned successfully#-' updates only the status message.
3.  If outputs are defined in the Formatted Outputs field, values are extracted from stdio when they follow this format:\
    '-#OUTPUT:VARIABLE_NAME--#--VARIABLE_VALUE#-'\
    For example: '-#OUTPUT:size--#--120568#-' populates @outputs\['size'\] = 120568 when an output of type TYPE_INT is defined.
4.  Stdio lines containing formatted output or status markers are removed from the stdio or multiline output when Formatted Outputs are configured.

### Media Mate Operation

![Media Mate Operation icon](icons/MediaMateOperation.png){.plugin-icon}

This action plugin can be used to interact with the MediaMate subtitling service.

| Version | Comment                                                |
|---------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| 0.3.0   | Updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8    |
| 0.2.5   | Fix issue with submit operation timing                 |
| 0.2.4   | Make Submit call Open rather than Run                  |
| 0.2.3   | Correct error handling                                 |
| 0.2.2   | Use model_path for Run and Submit                      |
| 0.2.1   | Correct synchronousness of plugin operations           |
| 0.2.0   | Provide better error handling                          |
| 0.1.0   | Adding Submit operation and configuration for Open/Run |
| 0.0.1   | Initial release                                        |

- - ### Description

    #### Saved Parameters Description

  - **Name**: The name used to identify a saved MediaMateOperation configured instance.

  - **Comments**: Some comments about this saved MediaMateOperation configured instance.

  - **Server**: The server on which MediaMate is running. This will be used to build the URL to make REST calls. The URL will be formatted as: http://\<server\>:\<port\>/\<url_path\>/.

  - **Port**: The port on which MediaMate is running. The default value is 8080. This will be used to build the URL to make REST calls. The URL will be formatted as: http://\<server\>:\<port\>/\<url_path\>/.

  - **URL path**: The path component of the URL which will be used to make REST calls. The URL will be formatted as: http://\<server\>:\<port\>/\<url_path\>/. The operation portion of the path (e.g. open, run, stop, or close) does not need to be included in this parameter as it will be added automatically based on what is chosen for the *Operation* parameter.

  - **Operation**: The operation to be performed. Currently supports:
    - Open - Load the FLX file and create all the required objects
    - Run - Start running a MediaMate workflow model
    - Stop - Stop MediaMate workflow model
    - Close - Closes MediaMate workflow model
    - Submit - Runs the MediaMate workflow model and closes it once the model is complete

  - **Xmlcfg**: Used for the Open and Submit operations, this parameter is the XML configuration as in MediaMate FLOXE. Make sure the structure is \<XMLCFG\>\<ComponentList\>...\</XMLCFG\>\</ComponentList\>.

  - **Model path**: Used for the Open and Submit operation, this parameter is the FLX file path of the MediaMate workflow model.

  - **Polling frequency**: Used for the Run and Submit operations, this parameter is the number of seconds Orchestrator waits before polling the MediaMate server on the status of the MediaMate workflow model. The default value is five seconds.

##### Outputs description

- **Full Response**: A hash of the entire response received from MediaMate.
- **Short Name**: The MediaMate short name.
- **Job State**: The state value of the running model.
- **Job State Description**: An explanation of the Job State as provided by MediaMate.
- **MediaMate Error**: An explanation of the error returned by the MediaMate server as provided by MediaMate. If no error is returned, the output will be 'Success'.
- **FLX Player State Value**: An output for the Run and Submit operations, this output is the state value of the FLX player.
- **Error Message**: If the plugin encounters an error, this output will be an explanation of that error.

##### Operating Instructions

Per current implementation of MediaMate API, Running, Stopping, and Closing operations will always perform action on model with id of 1. This is the same id that the Open operation always returns.

The Submit operation is actually a combination of the Open + Run + Stop + Close operations.

Only the Run and Submit operations are polling MediaMate periodically for a job status. The other operations are executing synchronously.

Use a short name in the URL to distinguish between concurrent job requests (e.g. mm01 in mediamate/mm01/interface/run/).

### Mongodb operation

![Mongodb operation icon](icons/MongodbOperation.png){.plugin-icon}

This action plugin enables interaction with MongoDB databases, supporting document operations (insert, find, update, remove), advanced queries, aggregation pipelines, map-reduce processing, and database administration commands for comprehensive data management and analysis.

| Version | Comment |
|----|----|
| 1.3.1 | Update mongo gem, updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8 |
| 1.3.0 | Remove hardcoded aspera directory reference, Updating to standard coding conventions |
| 1.2.3 | Removing Windows support code |
| 1.2.2 | Make database operation an optional input |
| 1.2.1 | Added description of how to encode urls with special chars. |
| 1.2.0 | fixed raw HTML being displayed |
| 1.1.9 | Fixed typo for elsif condition. |
| 1.1.8 | Fixes for multiple host in URL and client options not being accepted |
| 1.1.7 | Added styling to parameter help text. |
| 1.1.6 | Accepts JSON or Hash |
| 1.1.5 | Inserts DB name into URI if not provided |
| 1.1.4 | Fixes document to translate into hash |
| 1.1.3 | Added help file |
| 1.1.2 | Removes GEM check. Reduces logging |
| 1.1.1 | Fixes logging reset |
| 1.1.0 | Added Logging Option |
| 1.0.0 | Initial Version |

### How It Works

- Provides comprehensive MongoDB database operations including document queries, insertions, updates, deletions, aggregations, map-reduce operations, and database commands.
- Supports connection to MongoDB instances using standard connection URIs with authentication, host, port, and database specifications.
- Enables flexible operation execution with configurable client options, database options, and collection-specific parameters for customized database interactions.
- Offers multiple document manipulation operations including find, find_one, find_and_modify, insert, update, remove, save, group, aggregate, and advanced map-reduce operations.
- Returns structured output data including document counts, individual documents, document IDs, operation responses, and result arrays based on the operation type.
- Includes optional logging capabilities for monitoring and debugging MongoDB operations within workflow executions.

### Configuration Instructions

1.  `Add a `**`MongoDB Operation`**` step to your workflow.`
2.  `Configure the database connection:`
    - **`Connection uri`**`: Enter MongoDB connection string (e.g., mongodb://203.0.113.100:27017).`
    - **`Database name`**`: Specify the target database name (e.g., inventory_demo).`
    - **`Client options`**`: Add MongoDB client configuration options if needed.`
3.  `Set up the operation:`
    - **`Operation`**`: Select the MongoDB operation type (Document_find, Document_insert, Document_aggregate, etc.).`
    - **`Collection name`**`: Specify the target collection for document operations.`
    - **`Database options`**`: Configure database-specific parameters.`
    - **`Logging enabled`**`: Check to enable MongoDB server logging.`

### Example Configuration

| Parameter            | Example Value                               |
|----------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| **Connection uri**   | mongodb://dbuser:dbpass@203.0.113.100:27017 |
| **Database name**    | inventory_demo                              |
| **Operation**        | Document_find                               |
| **Collection name**  | products                                    |
| **Client options**   | {ssl: true, connectTimeoutMS: 30000}        |
| **Database options** | {readPreference: 'secondary'}               |
| **Logging enabled**  | checked                                     |

### Saved Parameters Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| **Connection uri** | The connection URI containing the host, port, credentials, and other connection parameters. Format: mongodb://\[username:password@\]host1\[:port1\]\[,host2\[:port2\],...\[,hostN\[:portN\]\]\]\[/\[database\]\[?options\]\] |
| **Client options** | Option parameters that can be applied to the MongoDB client for connection configuration and behavior customization. |
| **Database name** | Name of the database that needs to be connected to for the operation. |
| **Database options** | Option parameters that can be applied to the MongoDB Database for specific database-level configurations. |
| **Logging enabled** | This checkbox should be checked if logging needs to be enabled on the MongoDB server for operation monitoring. |
| **Operation** | Operation that needs to be performed on the MongoDB server. Available options are Database_command, Document_aggregate, Document_count, Document_distinct, Document_find, Document_find_and_modify, Document_find_one, Document_group, Document_insert, Document_map_reduce, Document_remove, Document_save and Document_update. |

### Inputs Description

Input parameters are not mandatory. URI defaults to 'mongodb://localhost:27017' and database name defaults to 'test'. The [MongoDB client documentation](http://www.rubydoc.info/github/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/Mongo/Client) lists all valid options that can be used with the MongoDB client.

#### Operation-Specific Inputs:

##### Database_command

- **Selector**: Database command specification. Example: {count: "products", query: {name: "sample_item"}}.
- **Options**: Options are parameters that are used with the database command.

##### Document_aggregate

- **Collection name**: Name of the collection in the MongoDB database.
- **Collection options**: Option parameters that can be applied to the MongoDB collection.
- **Pipeline**: Aggregation pipeline array. Example: \[{ "\$match": { "company": { "\$exists": true } } }, { "\$group": { "\_id": { "name": "\$name", "company": "\$company" }, "count": { "\$sum": 1 } } }\].
- **Options**: Aggregation operation options.

##### Document_find

- **Collection name**: Name of the collection in the MongoDB database.
- **Collection options**: Option parameters that can be applied to the MongoDB collection.
- **Selector**: Query selectors that are used to query the collection. Example: {"name" =\> "sample_product"}.
- **Options**: Query options such as limit, sort, projection. Example: {limit: 10, sort: {name: 1}}.

##### Document_find_one

- **Collection name**: Name of the collection in the MongoDB database.
- **Collection options**: Option parameters that can be applied to the MongoDB collection.
- **Selector**: Query selectors for finding a single document. Example: {"name" =\> "specific_item"}.
- **Options**: Query options for the find operation.

##### Document_find_and_modify

- **Collection name**: Name of the collection in the MongoDB database.
- **Collection options**: Option parameters that can be applied to the MongoDB collection.
- **Selector**: Query criteria for selecting the document. Example: {"name" =\> "item_to_modify"}.
- **Document**: The modification to apply. Example: {"name" =\> "updated_item"}.
- **Options**: Options for the find and modify operation.

##### Document_group

- **Collection name**: Name of the collection in the MongoDB database.
- **Collection options**: Option parameters that can be applied to the MongoDB collection.
- **Selector**: Query selectors that are used to query the collection for grouping operation.
- **Options**: Group operation options. Example: {"key": "name", "cond": {"name": {"\$exists": true}}, "aggregations": {"count": {"op": "\$sum", "field": 1}}}.

##### Document_insert

- **Collection name**: Name of the collection in the MongoDB database.
- **Collection options**: Option parameters that can be applied to the MongoDB collection.
- **Documents**: Document object(s) that need to be inserted. Example: {"name":"sample_product"} or for multiple: \[{"name": "product1"}, {"name": "product2"}\].
- **Options**: Insert operation options.

##### Document_map_reduce

- **Collection name**: Name of the collection in the MongoDB database.
- **Collection options**: Option parameters that can be applied to the MongoDB collection.
- **Map_function**: JavaScript map function. Example: function() { if (this.name && this.qty) { emit(this.name, this.qty); } }.
- **Reduce_function**: JavaScript reduce function. Example: function(key, values) { return Array.sum(values); }.
- **Options**: Map-reduce operation options.

##### Document_remove

- **Collection name**: Name of the collection in the MongoDB database.
- **Collection options**: Option parameters that can be applied to the MongoDB collection.
- **Selector**: Query selectors for selecting documents to remove. Example: {"name": "item_to_remove"}.
- **Allow Empty Selector?**: Check this checkbox if the selector is required to be empty. NOTE: This will remove all documents in the collection.
- **Options**: Remove operation options.

##### Document_update

- **Collection name**: Name of the collection in the MongoDB database.
- **Collection options**: Option parameters that can be applied to the MongoDB collection.
- **Selector**: Query criteria for selecting documents to update. Example: {"name" =\> "old_value"}.
- **Documents**: The update document that specifies modifications. Example: {"name" =\> "new_value"}.
- **Options**: Update operation options such as upsert, multi.

### Outputs Description

- **Count**: Count of all the documents that are returned from the selector query on the collection. Populated only for Document_count operation.
- **Document**: Hash of the document that is returned upon querying the database. Populated only for Document_find_and_modify and Document_find_one operations.
- **Document_id**: ID of the document returned. Populated only for Document_insert and Document_save operations.
- **Response**: Response hash of the operation. Populated only for Database_command, Document_map_reduce, Document_remove and Document_update operations.
- **Results**: Array of the documents that are returned performing operation. Populated only for Document_aggregate, Document_distinct, Document_find and Document_group operations.

### Supported Operations

- Database_command - Execute administrative commands on the database
- Document_aggregate - Perform aggregation pipeline operations on collections
- Document_count - Count documents matching query criteria
- Document_distinct - Get distinct values for a field across documents
- Document_find - Query and retrieve multiple documents from collections
- Document_find_and_modify - Find and modify documents in a single atomic operation
- Document_find_one - Query and retrieve a single document from collections
- Document_group - Group documents by specified criteria with aggregation functions
- Document_insert - Insert new documents into collections
- Document_map_reduce - Perform map-reduce operations on collections
- Document_remove - Delete documents from collections
- Document_save - Save documents (insert or update based on ID)
- Document_update - Modify existing documents in collections

### Operating Instructions

- **MongoDB Server Requirement:** This plugin is used to interact with MongoDB. A MongoDB database server must be installed and running, and credentials to access it should be available.
- **Connection Configuration:** Use the standard MongoDB connection URI format to specify host, port, database, and authentication details. The plugin defaults to 'mongodb://localhost:27017' if no URI is provided.
- **Documentation Reference:** For detailed information on options and selectors, refer to the MongoDB Ruby driver documentation at http://www.rubydoc.info/github/mongodb/mongo-ruby-driver/Mongo.
- **Query Limitations:** Some complex selector formats are not supported. For example, this selector for a Document_find operation is not supported: { "product.id": 12345 }, { "product.id": 1 }. It's supposed to retrieve only the id field in the document with id 12345. Use this form instead: { "product.id": 12345 } and then use a custom Ruby plugin to extract only the fields you need from the Results output.
- **Map-Reduce Operations:** When using Document_map_reduce, provide JavaScript functions for both Map_function and Reduce_function. Example Map function: function() { if (this.name && this.qty) { emit(this.name, this.qty); } }. Example Reduce function: function(key, values) { return Array.sum(values); }.
- **Multiple Document Insertion:** For Document_insert operations, you can create multiple inserts by providing an array format: \[{"name": "item1"}, {"name": "item2"}\]. After insertion, verify the records have been created in the collection.
- **Find and Modify Operations:** Document_find_and_modify performs atomic find and update operations. After entering details, select find_one_modify and update it to execute the operation.
- **Database Commands:** For Database_command operations, use the Selector field to specify the command. Example for counting documents: {count: "collection_name", query: {name: "specific_value"}}.
- **Aggregation Pipeline:** For Document_aggregate operations, provide the aggregation pipeline in the Pipeline field. Example: \[{ "\$match": { "company": { "\$exists": true } } }, { "\$group": { "\_id": { "name": "\$name", "company": "\$company" }, "count": { "\$sum": 1 } } }\].
- **Group Operations:** For Document_group operations, configure the Options field with grouping criteria. Example: {"key": "name", "cond": {"name": {"\$exists": true}}, "aggregations": {"count": {"op": "\$sum", "field": 1}}}.
- **Data Safety:** When using Document_remove with 'Allow Empty Selector?', be extremely cautious as this will remove ALL documents in the collection. Always verify your selector criteria before execution.

### Connection URI Format

- **Basic Format:** `mongodb://[username:password@]host1[:port1][,host2[:port2],...[,hostN[:portN]]][/[database][?options]]`
- **Simple Connection:** `mongodb://203.0.113.100:27017`
- **With Authentication:** `mongodb://dbuser:dbpass@203.0.113.100:27017/inventory_demo`
- **Replica Set:** `mongodb://203.0.113.100:27017,203.0.113.101:27017/inventory_demo?replicaSet=myReplSet`
- **With Options:** `mongodb://203.0.113.100:27017/inventory_demo?ssl=true&connectTimeoutMS=30000`
- **Character Escaping:** The characters ':' and '@' in a username or password must be escaped (RFC 2396).

### Example Use Cases

##### Case 1: Map-Reduce Analysis

- **Purpose:** Aggregate quantity data by product name across a collection
- **Map Function:** `function() { if (this.name && this.qty) { emit(this.name, this.qty); } }`
- **Reduce Function:** `function(key, values) { return Array.sum(values); }`
- **Result:** Returns aggregated quantities for each product name

##### Case 2: Document Operations

- **Insert:** Add new products with `{"name":"sample_product"}`
- **Update:** Modify existing records using selector `{"name" => "old_product"}` and document `{"name" => "updated_product"}`
- **Find:** Query documents with `{"name" => "specific_product"}`
- **Remove:** Delete documents matching `{"name": "product_to_remove"}`

##### Case 3: Advanced Operations

- **Aggregation:** Use pipeline `[{ "$match": { "company": { "$exists": true } } }, { "$group": { "_id": { "name": "$name", "company": "$company" }, "count": { "$sum": 1 } } }]`
- **Group:** Configure options `{"key": "name", "cond": {"name": {"$exists": true}}, "aggregations": {"count": {"op": "$sum", "field": 1}}}`
- **Find and Modify:** Atomic operations combining find with update in a single transaction

### Dependencies

- MongoDB server installed and running on the target host or accessible via network connection.
- Valid MongoDB database credentials with appropriate permissions for the intended operations.
- Network connectivity between Orchestrator and the MongoDB server on the specified port (default: 27017).
- MongoDB Ruby driver dependencies installed on the Orchestrator server.
- Proper firewall configurations allowing connections to MongoDB server ports.

### Troubleshooting

- If connection fails, verify that the MongoDB server is running and accessible at the specified host and port in the connection URI.
- For authentication errors, confirm that the username and password in the connection URI are correct and that the user has appropriate permissions for the target database.
- If operations timeout, check network connectivity and consider adjusting client options like connectTimeoutMS and socketTimeoutMS in the connection URI.
- For collection not found errors, ensure the collection exists or use operations that can create collections implicitly (such as insert operations).
- If selector queries return unexpected results, verify the query syntax matches MongoDB query format and test queries directly in MongoDB shell first.
- When Document_remove operations fail with empty selector, ensure 'Allow Empty Selector?' is checked if you intend to remove all documents, otherwise provide specific query criteria.
- For Map-Reduce operations, ensure JavaScript functions are syntactically correct and test them in MongoDB shell before using in the plugin.
- If Document_find_and_modify operations don't work as expected, verify that both selector and document fields contain valid MongoDB query and update specifications.
- For aggregation pipeline issues, validate the pipeline array syntax and ensure each stage follows MongoDB aggregation framework specifications.
- For group operations, verify the Options JSON structure includes proper key, condition, and aggregation specifications.
- For large result sets, consider using options like limit and skip to manage memory usage and improve performance.
- When using Database_command operations, ensure the command syntax follows MongoDB's database command format and includes all required parameters.

### Remote execution

![Remote execution icon](icons/RemoteExecution.png){.plugin-icon}

This action plugin provides the ability to execute scripts or executables on remote nodes via SSH, with support for dynamic input variables and custom output extraction.

| Version | Comment |
|----|----|
| 1.2.1 | Fix issues when using ERB template tags in command field |
| 1.2.0 | Updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8 |
| 1.1.2 | Avoid to set stdio in the detailed status if report stdio is set |
| 1.1.1 | Updating Edit panel and Help page |
| 1.1.0 | Add an option to remove the keep alive handling, causing issues on a Windows remote node |
| 1.0.0 | When a report stdio frequency is provided and return stdio option is selected, stdio is streamed to status and used as output. |
| 0.9.1 | Fixes help page |
| 0.9.0 | Implements cancel |
| 0.8.5 | Fixed raise condition on exit signal |
| 0.8.4 | Sets environment variable on the keep alive channel too |
| 0.8.3 | SSH close logic modified since it was taking more time. |
| 0.8.2 | Adding Remote Node Dependency |
| 0.8.1 | Clarifies the use of the 'use server address' features, by toggling sections. |
| 0.8.0 | Avoids closing before exit signal is received and ensures keep-alive channel is closed upon exit |
| 0.7.2 | Allows status changes in process output code block |
| 0.7.1 | New icon |
| 0.7.0 | Make server address as an optional field also. Requested by Turner 2014/11/14 |
| 0.6.4 | Split outputs processing into lines, in case buffer is not read line by line |
| 0.6.3 | Adds a default status exit message on success |
| 0.6.2 | -\> Improve help file |
| 0.6.1 | -\> exception handling of RemoteNode.find_by_name() |
| 0.6.0 | -\> Converted command type to text to allow for larger commands |
| 0.5.4 | -\> Avoids the exit race condition (channel closed before exit signal is processed) |
| 0.5.3 | -\> Fixing the version as it was still 0.5.1. Made the execution_node, execution_login and execution_password variables available for runtime. |
| 0.5.2 | -\> Prevents autocomplete of login field |
| 0.5.1 | -\> Added help |
| 0.5.0 | -\> Adds code execution for the formatting of outputs |
| 0.4.1 | -\> Fixes glitch with missing stdio |
| 0.4.0 | -\> Adds capture of exit code and user defined outputs |
| 0.3.1 | -\> Adds functionality to mask password |
| 0.3.0 | -\> When a report stderr frequency is provided, stderr is streamed to status instead of log file. |
| 0.2.0 | -\> When a report stdio frequency is provided, stdio is streamed to status instead of used as output. |
| 0.1.5 | -\> modified view to collapse optional execution account info |
| 0.1.4 | -\> Fixes erroneous return code |
| 0.1.3 | -\> Uses termination API for early exit |
| 0.1.2 | -\> Adds support for clustered nodes |
| 0.1.1 | -\> Forces out of the SSH.wait when a status is returned |
| 0.1.0 | -\> Makes the timeout a template parameter, SSH timeout is used only in the case of infinite template-defined timeout |
| 0.0.2 | -\> Adds keep alive and set timeout to ensure the action times-out if the client SSHD becomes unreachable. |
| 0.0.1 | -\> Working. Used for both executable and remote daemons |

### How It Works

- Executes scripts or executables on remote nodes via SSH connections with configurable timeout and cancellation support.
- Supports dynamic command line construction using input variables accessible via template syntax (\<%=variable_name%\>).
- Captures standard output, standard error, and exit codes from remote command execution.
- Provides multiline output processing and custom variable extraction from command output.
- Offers real-time status updates and progress reporting during remote execution.

### Configuration Instructions

1.  `Add a `**`RemoteExecution`**` step to your workflow.`
2.  `Configure the basic execution parameters:`
    - **`Name`**`: Enter a descriptive name for the step (e.g., 'New RemoteExecution Step').`
    - **`Command`**`: Enter the command line to execute (e.g., 'echo "Hello from Orchestrator" > /tmp/orch_test_hello.txt').`
    - **`Keep alive?`**`: Check to enable SSH keep-alive mechanism (recommended).`
3.  `Set timeout and cancellation options:`
    - **`Is cancelable?`**`: Check to allow cancellation of the remote execution.`
    - **`Timeout`**`: Leave empty to use the default of 60 seconds, or specify custom timeout.`
4.  `Configure advanced features (optional):`
    - **`Standard I/O`**`: Click to expand and configure stdout/stderr handling.`
    - **`Mandatory inputs`**`: Click 'Add Mandatory inputs' to define required variables.`
    - **`Optional inputs`**`: Click 'Add Optional inputs' for optional variables.`
    - **`Formatted Outputs`**`: Click 'Add Formatted Outputs' to extract custom variables.`

### Example Configuration

| Parameter | Example Value |
|----|----|
| **Name** | New RemoteExecution Step |
| **Command** | echo "Hello from Orchestrator" \> /tmp/orch_test_hello.txt |
| **Is cancelable?** | unchecked |
| **Keep alive?** | checked |
| **Standard I/O** | (collapsed - using defaults) |

#### Expected Test Results

Based on the above configuration, the plugin successfully:

- Executes the echo command on the remote node (RPM Build 1037)
- Creates the file /tmp/orch_test_hello.txt with content 'Hello from Orchestrator'
- Returns successful execution status
- Provides standard outputs: exit_code, stdio, and any configured custom outputs

### Saved Parameters Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| **Name** | Display name for this remote execution step. |
| **Comments** | Notes or description associated with this template or configuration. |
| **Command** | Command line to execute on the remote node. Use \<%=variable_name%\> syntax for dynamic variables. |
| **Is cancelable?** | When enabled, canceling will shut down the SSH connection, potentially stopping remote command execution. |
| **Keep alive?** | Enable internal keep-alive mechanism. Recommended ON for Linux; may be disabled for Windows to avoid extra processes. |
| **Timeout** | Maximum execution time in seconds before remote command is terminated. Leave empty to use 60-second default. |
