| **Standard I/O** | Configure stdout/stderr reporting frequency and multiline output handling. |
| **Mandatory inputs** | Define required input variables accessible in the command line via \<%=variable_name%\> notation. |
| **Optional inputs** | Define optional input variables accessible in the command line via \<%=variable_name%\> notation. |
| **Formatted outputs** | Specify custom output variables that the step will produce. |
| **Formatted outputs processing code** | Ruby code executed after command completion. Access outputs via @outputs\[VARIABLE_STDIO\], @outputs\[VARIABLE_MULTILINE\], @outputs\[VARIABLE_EXITCODE\]. |
| **Remote execution node** | Target remote node from the list of configured Remote Nodes. |
| **Execution server address** | Alternative remote node address to use instead of configured node address. |
| **Use server address** | Use the provided server address instead of the remote node's configured address. |
| **Execution account** | SSH username and password for remote connection. If empty, uses credentials specified in the remote node configuration. |

### Inputs Description

Runtime inputs depend on the configuration. Variables exposed in the command line become mandatory inputs.

- **Execution_node**: Remote node from which the command is executed.
- **Execution_login**: Login name to access the remote node (optional).
- **Execution_password**: Password to access the remote node (optional).
- **Custom variables**: Any variables defined in Mandatory inputs or Optional inputs sections.

### Outputs Description

- **exit_code**: Exit code of the remote execution. Non-zero values cause plugin to return an error.
- **stdio**: Standard output from the remote execution when multiline is set to false.
- **Multi**: Standard output returned as an array when multiline is enabled (one entry per output line).
- **Additional outputs**: Custom outputs defined in "Formatted outputs" section are also available.

### Supported Actions

None

### Dependencies

- SSH access must be configured and functional on target remote nodes.
- For environment variables over SSH, /etc/ssh/sshd_config must include AcceptEnv directives.
- Remote node must have required scripts/executables and proper permissions.

### Operating Instructions

#### Standard I/O Configuration

- **Empty stdio frequency:** Standard output is streamed to stdio output variable and plugin status message when execution completes.
- **With stdio frequency:** Standard output streams to plugin status message during execution. Final output goes to stdio variable if enabled.
- **With stderr frequency:** Standard error streams to plugin status message. Otherwise goes to Orchestrator log file.

#### Status Updates

Remote executables can provide status updates using this format:

**'-#STATUS--#--STATUS MESSAGE#-'**

- '-#Paused--#--Remote action was paused#-' → updates status and message
- '-#--#--File tester.txt was scanned successfully#-' → updates message only
- Shell script example: `echo "-#Paused--#--Remote action was paused#-"`

#### Output Variable Extraction

Extract custom variables from standard output using this format:

**'-#OUTPUT:VARIABLE NAME--#--VARIABLE VALUE#-'**

**Example:** '-#OUTPUT:size--#--120568#-' → populates @outputs\['size'\]=120568

#### Example Shell Script

Shell script returning error 2, output variable 'size', multiline true:

    md5sum $1 | cut -d' ' -f1
    echo "-#OUTPUT:size--#--120568#-"
    echo "hello"
    exit 2

**Plugin outputs (multiline true):**

    size      120568
    exit_code 2
    Multi     ["45c2d08009be4a2307eddd4f0bd10a8f", "hello"]

**Plugin outputs (multiline false):**

    size      120568
    exit_code 2
    stdio     45c2d08009be4a2307eddd4f0bd10a8f
              hello

#### Environment Variables

To enable environment variables over SSH, modify /etc/ssh/sshd_config:

1.  Add line: **AcceptEnv \[env_variable_1\] \[env_variable_2\] \[...\]**
2.  Restart sshd service for changes to take effect
3.  On Isilon systems, file location is /etc/mcp/templates/sshd_config

### REST request

![REST request icon](icons/RestRequest.png){.plugin-icon}

This plugin allows you to make REST requests to web services, either as a one-time operation or with repeated polling at specified intervals.

| Version | Comment |
|----|----|
| 1.2.3 | Fix post_parameters_get to convert String to Hash |

| 1.1.6 | Fix type conversion when headers parameters are passed at runtime |
| 1.1.5 | Fix result in case of a Json array |
| 1.1.4 | Added option for patch method |
| 1.1.3 | Fixed error in case of get request with empty headers parameters |
| 1.1.2 | Added protection against null headers parameters |
| 1.1.1 | Updating configuration and help pages. |
| 1.1.0 | Adding flexibility to specify ssl version. |
| 1.0.9 | Add some json_result examples in the help |
| 1.0.8 | Proxy is applied per request and not globally. |
| 1.0.7 | Ensure proxy is applied when polling. |

| 0.0.1 | Only supports POST command |

### How It Works

- Generates REST requests to remote services and handles responses with support for GET, POST, DELETE, and PUT methods.
- Supports multiple authentication methods including basic authentication and SSL certificate authentication.
- Provides polling capabilities to repeatedly execute requests until conditions are met.
- Offers trigger functionality to spawn new work orders when new records are detected in responses.
- Handles various content types (XML, JSON, text) with automatic parsing and formatting capabilities.

### Configuration Instructions

1.  `Add a `**`RestRequest`**` step to your workflow.`
2.  `Configure the basic request parameters:`
    - **`Name`**`: Enter a descriptive name for the step (e.g., 'New RestRequest Step').`
    - **`Service URL`**`: HTTP URL to reach the remote service (e.g., https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/1).`
    - **`REST method`**`: Select GET, POST, DELETE, or PUT from the dropdown.`
    - **`Response type`**`: Choose json, xml, or text based on expected response format.`
3.  `Configure SSL and security settings:`
    - **`SSL verification`**`: Check to enable SSL verification (recommended for HTTPS URLs).`
    - **`Ssl version`**`: Select appropriate SSL version or leave as default.`
    - **`Basic Auth login/password`**`: Leave empty if authentication is not required.`
4.  `Set optional parameters:`
    - **`Request timeout`**`: Leave empty for no timeout or specify seconds.`
    - **`Return Code for Network failure`**`: Leave empty to use default -1.`
    - **`Save output to file`**`: Check only if you need to save response to a file.`

### Example Configuration

| Parameter                  | Example Value                                |
|----------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| **Name**                   | New RestRequest Step                         |
| **Comments**               | (empty)                                      |
| **Service URL**            | https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/1 |
| **Rest method**            | GET                                          |
| **Basic Auth login**       | (empty)                                      |
| **Basic Auth password**    |                                              |
| **SSL verification**       | checked                                      |
| **SSL Version**            | -Select-                                     |
| **Response type**          | json                                         |
| **Request timeout**        | (default: no timeout)                        |
| **Save output to file**    | unchecked                                    |
| **Processed outputs code** | (empty)                                      |

### Saved Parameters Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| **Name** | Display name for this REST request step. |
| **Comments** | Notes or description for this step. |
| **Service URL** | HTTP URL to reach the remote service. |
| **Rest method** | REST method: GET, POST, DELETE, or PUT. |
| **Basic Auth login** | Account name for basic authentication (optional). |
| **Basic Auth password** | Account password for basic authentication (optional). |
| **SSL Client Certificate File Path** | Path to SSL client certificate file. |
| **SSL Client Key File Path** | Path to SSL client key file. |
| **SSL Client Key Passphrase** | Passphrase for SSL client certificate. |
| **SSL CA Certificate File Path** | Path to SSL CA certificate file. |
| **SSL Verification** | Enable SSL verification instead of login/password authentication. |
| **SSL Version** | SSL version to be used. |
| **Polling frequency** | Polling interval in seconds. If not set, no polling is performed. |
| **Polling stop condition code** | Ruby code condition to stop polling (default: return true). |
| **Continue Monitoring** | Use plugin as trigger. Requires non-null polling frequency. |
| **Maximum number of records returned** | Maximum records in response for Formatted_result output. Each spawned work order handles this maximum. |
| **Result records extracting code** | Ruby code to extract array from response. |
| **Record formatting code** | Ruby code to format individual records from extracted array. |
| **Record identification code** | Ruby code to identify unique records from formatted records (for trigger mode). |
| **Header parameters** | HTTP request header name/value pairs in Ruby Hash format: { 'key' =\> 'value' }. |
| **Content type** | Request content type: jpg, xml, gif, text, json, pdf (for POST/PUT requests). |
| **Read POST body from file?** | Read POST body content from file instead of text field. |
| **POST body file path** | Full path to file containing POST body content. |
| **HTTP form POST?** | Use HTTP form to build POST request body. |
| **POST parameters** | Name/value pairs for HTTP form parameters. |
| **POST request body** | POST body content matching defined content type. |
| **Response type** | Expected response content type: json, xml, or text. |
| **Request timeout** | Timeout duration in seconds before giving up on request (optional). |
| **Return Code for Network failure** | Custom return code for network failures (default: -1, empty: -100). |
| **Processed outputs** | Name/type pairs for additional plugin outputs. |
| **Processed outputs code** | Ruby code executed after response processing to populate processed outputs. |

### Inputs Description

Changes in this list of inputs will override any values set in the action template.

#### Mandatory Inputs:

- **Service_url**: HTTP URL to reach the remote service.
- **POST_parameters**: Name/value pairs for HTTP form parameters (if 'HTTP form POST?' is selected).
- **POST_body**: POST request body content (if 'HTTP form POST?' is not selected).

#### Optional Inputs:

- **Basic_auth_login**: Account name for basic authentication.
- **Basic_auth_password**: Account password for basic authentication.
- **Header_parameters**: HTTP request header parameters.
- **Processed_outputs_code**: Ruby code executed after response processing.
- **Polling_stop_condition**: Ruby code condition to stop polling.
- **Records_extracting_code**: Ruby code to extract array from response.
- **Record_formatting_code**: Ruby code to format records from extracted array.
- **PUT_file_path**: File path for PUT method when POST request body is not filled.
- **Save Output to file**: Check to save output to file.
- **Write response to this file**: File path where response will be written.

### Outputs Description

- **Service_result**: Raw response as a string.
- **Response_header**: HTTP header of the response as a hash.
- **XML_result**: XML parsed response as a hash (only present if response type is xml).
- **JSON_result**: JSON parsed response as a hash (only present if response type is json).
- **Formatted_result**: Formatted response as an array after extracting and formatting code have been applied.
- **HTTP_return_code**: HTTP status code returned by the service (e.g., 200 for success).
- **Processed outputs**: Each name/value pair defined in processed outputs creates a new output of the selected type.

### Supported Actions

- GET
- POST
- DELETE
- PUT

### Dependencies

None

### Operating Instructions

**Overview:** This action generates REST requests to remote services and handles responses.

#### URL Parameters and Authentication

- Parameters can be passed in the HTTP URL using ? and & notation: `http://localhost/api/endpoint?param1=value1&param2=value2`
- Parameters can alternatively be passed in the HTTP request body using POST method with either HTML form format or custom content.
- Authentication can be done directly in the URL or by specifying basic authentication credentials in dedicated fields.
- Basic authentication combines username:password, Base64 encodes it, and puts it in the HTTP Authorization header.

#### Content Types and Response Handling

- Specify content type for request body (xml, json, text, etc.) and expected response type.
- If **xml** response type is selected, response is parsed and 'XML_result' variable populated with hash.
- If **json** response type is selected, response is parsed and 'JSON_result' variable populated with hash.
- If **text** response type is selected, no parsing occurs and 'Service_result' variable holds raw response string.

#### Processed Outputs

Define name/type pairs for processed outputs and use "Processed outputs code" to populate them:

- Use syntax: `outputs['<variable_name>'] = <value>`
- XML example: `outputs['name'] = xml_result['name'][0]`
- JSON example: `outputs['name'] = json_result['name']`

#### Test Results Example

When calling a public REST API like JSONPlaceholder (https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/1) with GET method:

- **HTTP_return_code**: 200 (indicating successful request)
- **Service_result**: Raw JSON response as string
- **JSON_result**: Parsed JSON response as hash with fields like "userId", "id", "title", "body"
- **Formatted_result**: Empty array when no formatting code is specified

#### Polling Functionality

- Set the polling frequency (e.g., 5 seconds) to repeat requests until a condition is met.
- Define stop condition in Ruby using 'Polling stop condition code' field.
- Example XML stop condition: `xml_result['status'][0] == 'complete' rescue nil`
- Example JSON stop condition: `json_result['status'] == 'complete' rescue nil`

#### Trigger Functionality

- Select 'Continue Monitoring' to enable trigger mode that spawns new work orders for new records.
- Use 'Result records extracting code' to extract array from response (e.g., `return xml_result['workflow']`).
- Use 'Record formatting code' to format each record (often `record = record.to_s` for readability).
- Use 'Record identification code' to identify unique records (e.g., `return record['id']`).
- Records already detected are stored and won't be re-detected unless the workflow is restarted.
- Extracting and formatting code can be used without trigger mode for response formatting.

#### Example XML Response Processing

Given XML response:

    <?xml version="1.0"?>
    <workflows>
      <workflow id="1" name="create file"/>
      <workflow id="2" name="custom ruby"/>
    </workflows>

After XML parsing, the `xml_result` variable contains the following hash:

    { 'workflow' => [{ "name" => "create file", "id" => "1" }, { "name" => "custom ruby", "id" => "2" }] }

- **Result records extracting code:** `return xml_result['workflow']`
- **Record formatting code:** `return { "id" => record["id"], "name" => record["name"] }`
- **Record identification code:** `return record["id"]`

### Soap request

![Soap request icon](icons/SoapRequest.png){.plugin-icon}

This plug-in provides the ability to periodically execute a SOAP query on a web service. It can be used for a one-off request or as a polling mechanism.

### How It Works

- Generates SOAP requests to remote web services and processes their responses.
- Supports WSDL-based service discovery (remote URI or local file) or manual endpoint configuration.
- Provides polling capabilities for continuous monitoring and trigger-based workflow execution.
- Offers flexible response parsing with Ruby code for extracting, formatting, and processing data.
- Supports HTTP basic authentication and session management with cookies.

### Configuration Instructions

1.  `Add a `**`SoapRequest`**` step to your workflow.`
2.  `Configure the basic parameters:`
    - **`Name`**`: Enter a descriptive name for the SOAP request.`
    - **`Comments`**`: Add notes or a description for this step.`
3.  `Set up service connection (choose one method):`
    - **`Method 1 - WSDL URI`**`: Provide the WSDL URI to describe the remote service (e.g., http://example.com/service?wsdl).`
    - **`Method 2 - WSDL File`**`: Use a local WSDL file stored in /opt/aspera/orchestrator/config/orchestrator/actions/soap_requests`
    - **`Method 3 - Manual Configuration`**`: Specify both the endpoint and SOAP action directly (requires 'Provide explicit payload?' to be checked).`
4.  `Configure service operation:`
    - **`Service`**`: Select the SOAP operation from the drop-down list (populated from the WSDL).`
    - **`Provide explicit payload?`**`: Check to provide an explicit SOAP XML payload in the 'Service payload' box.`
    - **`Service payload`**`: Enter the complete SOAP XML payload (use <%= variable %> notation for dynamic values).`
    - **`Service action parameters`**`: Alternatively, provide operation parameters (if explicit payload is not used).`
    - **`Session header parameters`**`: Provide SOAP header parameters (if explicit payload not used).`
5.  `Configure authentication (optional):`
    - **`HTTP basic auth user`**`: Account name for HTTP basic authentication.`
    - **`HTTP basic auth password`**`: Account password for HTTP basic authentication.`
6.  `Set up polling and trigger behavior (optional):`
    - **`Polling frequency`**`: Interval in seconds for repeated requests. If not set, no polling is done.`
    - **`Continue Monitoring`**`: Enable trigger mode to spawn new workorders for each detected record.`
    - **`Maximum number of records returned`**`: Maximum records per response for Formatted_result output.`
    - **`Polling stop condition code`**`: Ruby code to determine when to stop polling.`
    - **`Record identification code`**`: Ruby code to identify unique records (required with 'Continue Monitoring').`
7.  `Configure response processing:`
    - **`Use legacy XML parsing?`**`: Use XmlSimple library instead of Nokogiri (default since v1.7.2).`
    - **`Result records extracting code`**`: Ruby code to extract an array from the response.`
    - **`Record formatting code`**`: Ruby code to format each extracted record.`
    - **`Results formatting code`**`: Ruby code executed after response processing.`
    - **`Result type`**`: Expected type of Service_results output (array, string, int, hash, flag).`
    - **`Return raw response?`**`: Include full XML response with SOAP envelope.`
    - **`Return structured response body?`**`: Include XML response body as structured data.`
    - **`Return real status`**`: Allow modifying return status in formatting/extraction code.`
8.  `Enable debugging (optional):`
    - **`Enable wsdl debug log file?`**`: Store SOAP message exchange copy under run directory.`

### Example Configuration

| Parameter | Example Value |
|----|----|
| **Name** | Get Database Records |
| **Comments** | Retrieves records from remote database service |
| **Wsdl uri** | http://www.example.com/ws/services/DBService?wsdl |
| **Service** | fetchRecords |
| **Provide explicit payload?** | checked |
| **Service payload** | \<fetchRecords\>\<query\>\<%= query_param %\>\</query\>\</fetchRecords\> |
| **HTTP basic auth user** | api_user |
| **HTTP basic auth password** | \*\*\*\*\*\*\*\* |
| **Polling frequency** | 30 |
| **Continue Monitoring** | checked |
| **Maximum number of records returned** | 100 |
| **Use legacy XML parsing?** | unchecked |
| **Result type** | array |
| **Return structured response body?** | checked |

### Saved Parameters Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| **Name** | Display name for this SOAP request instance. |
| **Comments** | Notes or description for this step. |
| **Wsdl uri** | The WSDL URI to describe the remote service (e.g., http://example.com/service?wsdl). |
| **Wsdl file** | Local WSDL file stored in /opt/aspera/orchestrator/config/orchestrator/actions/soap_requests. |
| **Enable wsdl debug log file?** | If selected, copies of SOAP message exchanges are stored in the run directory. |
| **Service** | The SOAP operation from the defined operations for the remote service. |
| **Provide explicit payload?** | Check to provide an explicit SOAP XML payload in the 'Service payload' box. |
| **End point** | The URL for the remote node providing the service. Can be used if no WSDL is specified. |
| **SOAP action** | Explicit SOAP operation. Can be used if no WSDL is specified. |
| **HTTP basic auth user** | Account name for HTTP basic authentication. Optional. |
| **HTTP basic auth password** | Account password for HTTP basic authentication. Optional. |
| **Service payload** | The SOAP request XML payload. Supports variable notation \<%= variable %\>. |
| **Service action parameters** | SOAP request operation parameters if explicit payload is not provided. |
| **Session header parameters** | SOAP request header parameters if explicit payload is not provided. |
| **Polling frequency** | Polling interval in seconds. If not set, no polling is done. |
| **Continue Monitoring** | Enable trigger mode. Requires non-null polling frequency. |
| **Maximum number of records returned** | Maximum records in a response for the Formatted_result output. |
| **Use legacy XML parsing?** | Use XmlSimple library instead of Nokogiri (default since v1.7.2). |
| **Polling stop condition code** | Ruby code used to determine when to stop polling. Default: return true (stop now). |
| **Record identification code** | Ruby code to identify unique records from formatted records. Required with 'Continue Monitoring'. |
| **Return real status** | Allow modifying return status and status_details in formatting/extraction code. By default, step returns COMPLETE unless critical error detected. |
| **Result records extracting code** | Ruby code to extract an array from the response. |
| **Record formatting code** | Ruby code to format records from the extracted array. |
| **Results formatting code** | Ruby code executed after response processing. |
| **Result type** | Expected type of Service_results output: array, string, int, hash, or flag. |
| **Return raw response?** | Include full XML response with SOAP envelope in Raw_response output. |
| **Return structured response body?** | Include XML response body as structured data in Response_body output. |

### Inputs Description

Changes in this list of inputs will override any values set in the action template.

**Mandatory Inputs:**

- **Wsdl_uri**: The WSDL URI to describe the remote service. Not needed if End point and SOAP action are specified.
- **Service_action**: The SOAP operation from the defined operations. Not needed if End point and SOAP action are specified.

**Optional Inputs:**

- **Http_basic_auth_user**: Account name for HTTP basic authentication.
- **Http_basic_auth_password**: Account password for HTTP basic authentication.
- **Result_formatting_code**: Ruby code executed after the response is treated.
- **Polling_stop_condition**: Ruby code condition to stop the polling.
- **Records_extracting_code**: Ruby code to extract an array from the response.
- **Record_formatting_code**: Ruby code to format records from the extracted array.
- **Cookie**: HTTP header cookie to maintain session information between multiple SOAP requests.

### Outputs Description

- **Raw_response**: The raw response as a string. Only present if 'Return raw response?' is selected.
- **Response_body**: The SOAP response body as a hash. Only present if 'Return structured response body?' is selected.
- **Service_results**: The results after the code in 'Results formatting code' has been applied. Type is determined by 'Result type'. By default an array.
- **Cookie**: The cookie response as a string for session management.

**Note:** Each processed output name/value pair will introduce a new output of the selected type.

### Supported Actions

None

### Dependencies

- Network connectivity to the remote SOAP service endpoint.
- Valid WSDL file (either remote URI or local file) or manual endpoint/action configuration.
- For local WSDL files: File must be stored in /opt/aspera/orchestrator/config/orchestrator/actions/soap_requests
- Ruby environment for custom code execution (extracting, formatting, stop conditions).

### Operating Instructions

#### Overview

The SoapRequest plugin generates SOAP requests to remote web services and handles their responses. It supports WSDL-based service discovery, manual endpoint configuration, polling for continuous monitoring, and flexible response processing with Ruby code.

#### Service Configuration Methods

- **Method 1 - Remote WSDL URI**: Provide an HTTP URI to the WSDL (e.g., http://www.ebi.ac.uk/ws/services/WSDbfetch?wsdl). The plugin will parse the WSDL and populate available operations in the Service drop-down.
- **Method 2 - Local WSDL File**: Store the WSDL file in /opt/aspera/orchestrator/config/orchestrator/actions/soap_requests and select it from the drop-down. Useful for offline development or when the remote WSDL is unstable.
- **Method 3 - Manual Configuration**: Specify both End point and SOAP action directly. This requires checking 'Provide explicit payload?' and entering the complete XML payload manually. Use this when no WSDL is available.

#### Payload Configuration

##### Explicit Payload Mode (Recommended)

- Check the 'Provide explicit payload?' box to enter XML manually.
- Test your SOAP request with tools like SoapUI first, then copy and paste the XML into the 'Service payload' field.
- Use variable notation \<%= variable_name %\> for dynamic values.
- This method is more predictable than relying on parameter parsing from WSDL.

**Example Payload:**

    <fetchRecords>
      <database><%= database_name %></database>
      <query><%= search_query %></query>
      <maxResults>100</maxResults>
    </fetchRecords>

##### Parameter Mode (Alternative)

- Leave 'Provide explicit payload?' unchecked and use 'Service action parameters' or 'Session header parameters'.
- Not recommended as it relies on WSDL parsing which can be unpredictable.
- The plugin attempts to build the SOAP request from the parameters and WSDL structure.
- Use explicit payload mode for more control and reliability.

#### Authentication

- HTTP Basic Authentication is supported via username and password fields.
- Credentials are combined as "username:password", Base64 encoded, and sent in the Authorization header.
- Leave fields empty if authentication is not required.
- For more complex authentication (e.g., WS-Security), include authentication elements in your explicit payload.

#### XML Parsing Modes

**IMPORTANT:** For all configurations defined before version 2.0 of the plugin, legacy mode is the default. For new configurations, legacy mode will not be selected as the default.

##### Modern Mode (Nokogiri - Default since v1.7.2)

Example response structure:

    {"GetSessionsResponse"=>{"xmlns:ns1"=>"urn:Aspera:XML:FASPSessionNET:2009/11:Types",
      "SessionKeysResult"=>{"ResultCount"=>"2", "RemainingResultCount"=>"0",
        "IterationToken"=>"dd549761f97954417d2abf3c47ec877b0000000000001229",
        "SessionKey"=>[{"UUID"=>"fe77385c-3d17-4613-b839-1fc04bc5d423"},
                       {"UUID"=>"678357a5-cbf1-490a-8417-3d1f17390ea6"}]}}}

##### Legacy Mode (XmlSimple)

Example response structure:

    {"GetSessionsResponse"=>[{"SessionKeysResult"=>[{"ResultCount"=>["2"],
      "SessionKey"=>[{"UUID"=>["fe77385c-3d17-4613-b839-1fc04bc5d423"]},
                     {"UUID"=>["678357a5-cbf1-490a-8417-3d1f17390ea6"]}],
      "RemainingResultCount"=>["0"],
      "IterationToken"=>["dd549761f97954417d2abf3c47ec877b0000000000001229"]}],
      "xmlns:ns1"=>"urn:Aspera:XML:FASPSessionNET:2009/11:Types"}]}

**Note:** The structure differs significantly between modes. Adjust your Ruby code accordingly.

#### Polling and Stop Conditions

- Set a 'Polling frequency' (in seconds) to enable repeated requests.
- The SOAP request will repeat until the stop condition is met.
- Define stop condition in 'Polling stop condition code' using Ruby.
- The `xml_result` variable contains the parsed XML body.

**Example Stop Condition (Modern Mode):**

    xml_result["GetSessionsResponse"]["SessionKeysResult"]["RemainingResultCount"] == '0' rescue nil

**Example Stop Condition (Legacy Mode):**

    xml_result["GetSessionsResponse"][0]["SessionKeysResult"][0]["RemainingResultCount"][0] == '0' rescue nil

#### Trigger Mode and Record Processing

Enable 'Continue Monitoring' to spawn new workorders for each detected record.

- Requires 'Polling frequency' to be set.
- Use 'Result records extracting code' to extract an array from the response.
- Use 'Record formatting code' to format each extracted record.
- Use 'Record identification code' to identify unique records.
- Each detected record spawns a new workorder instance.
- Previously detected records are memorized and won't re-trigger (unless workflow is restarted).

#### Response Processing Examples

**Note:** The following examples assume legacy XML parsing mode. Adapt for modern mode by adjusting array indices.

##### Example SOAP Response

    <soapenv:Envelope xmlns:soapenv="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/envelope/">
      <soapenv:Body>
        <ns1:GetSessionsResponse xmlns:ns1="urn:Aspera:XML:FASPSessionNET:2009/11:Types">
          <SessionKeysResult>
            <ResultCount>2</ResultCount>
            <RemainingResultCount>0</RemainingResultCount>
            <IterationToken>dd549761f97954417d2abf3c47ec877b0000000000001229</IterationToken>
            <SessionKey>
              <UUID>fe77385c-3d17-4613-b839-1fc04bc5d423</UUID>
            </SessionKey>
            <SessionKey>
              <UUID>678357a5-cbf1-490a-8417-3d1f17390ea6</UUID>
            </SessionKey>
          </SessionKeysResult>
        </ns1:GetSessionsResponse>
      </soapenv:Body>
    </soapenv:Envelope>

##### Result Records Extracting Code

**Modern Mode:**

    return xml_result["GetSessionsResponse"]["SessionKeysResult"]["SessionKey"]

This extracts:

    [{"UUID"=>"fe77385c-3d17-4613-b839-1fc04bc5d423"},
     {"UUID"=>"678357a5-cbf1-490a-8417-3d1f17390ea6"}]

##### Record Formatting Code

    return { "id" => record["UUID"] } rescue nil

Service_results output:

    [{"id" => "fe77385c-3d17-4613-b839-1fc04bc5d423"},
     {"id" => "678357a5-cbf1-490a-8417-3d1f17390ea6"}]

##### Record Identification Code

    return "#{record["id"]}"

This identifies each record by its UUID for trigger tracking.

##### Simple Formatting Alternative

If you only need the UUID values:

    return record["UUID"]

Service_results output:

    ["fe77385c-3d17-4613-b839-1fc04bc5d423",
     "678357a5-cbf1-490a-8417-3d1f17390ea6"]

In this case, 'Record identification code' can be left empty as each record is already a unique identifier.

#### Results Formatting Code

For post-processing after extraction and formatting:

    token = xml_result["GetSessionsResponse"]["SessionKeysResult"]["IterationToken"]
    results = { "iteration_token" => token }

Change 'Result type' to 'hash'. Service_results output:

    {"iteration_token"=>"91286e2557483993505661edef5435690000000000001234"}

#### Debugging Tips

- **Inspect xml_result structure**: Use `return xml_result.inspect` in 'Result records extracting code' to see the full structure.
- **Enable debug logging**: Check 'Enable wsdl debug log file?' to save SOAP exchanges for inspection.
- **Test with SoapUI**: Validate your SOAP requests externally before implementing in the plugin.
- **Use rescue clauses**: Always include `rescue nil` in Ruby code to prevent errors from stopping workflows.
- **Check Response_body output**: Enable 'Return structured response body?' to see the parsed SOAP body structure.

#### Session Management

- Use the Cookie input and output for maintaining sessions across multiple SOAP requests.
- The Cookie output from one request can be mapped to the Cookie input of subsequent requests.
- This allows stateful interactions with services that require session management.
- Cookies are returned as strings and should be passed without modification.

#### Best Practices

- Use explicit payload mode for better control and reliability.
- Test SOAP requests with SoapUI or similar tools before implementing in workflows.
- For new configurations, use modern XML parsing mode (Nokogiri) for cleaner data structures.
- Always include `rescue nil` or proper error handling in Ruby code.
- Use 'Enable wsdl debug log file?' during development to troubleshoot request/response issues.
- Keep polling frequencies reasonable to avoid overwhelming the remote service.
- When using trigger mode, ensure 'Record identification code' uniquely identifies records.
- Store sensitive credentials securely and use variable notation in payloads.
- Document your Ruby extraction and formatting code for future maintenance.
- Consider using 'Maximum number of records returned' to limit memory usage with large responses.

### Softlayer API

![Softlayer API icon](icons/SoftlayerApi.png){.plugin-icon}

Integration with Softlayer API

| Version | Comment                                                    |
|---------|------------------------------------------------------------|
| 0.2.2   | Updated the label from Keep ongoing to Continue Monitoring |
| 0.2.1   | Updated the label from Keep ongoing to Continue Monitoring |
| 0.2.0   | Updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8        |
| 0.1.2   | added SoftLayer_Network_StorageAdded                       |
| 0.1.1   | Fixed ORC-2546 where payload is not read from mapped input |
| 0.1.0   | Added result limit and result offset                       |
| 0.0.1   | Initial version                                            |

#### How It Works

- This action plug-in integrates with the [Softlayer API](https://sldn.softlayer.com/article/rest/).

- SoftLayer uses a fixed API endpoint; it cannot be changed.

- You must provide a SoftLayer username and API key (called the Classic Infrastructure API Key in IBM Cloud).

- The list of available SoftLayer services is documented in the [Softlayer Developer Network](https://sldn.softlayer.com/reference/softlayerapi/).

- - If the polling frequency is greater than 0, the API URL is called periodically based on the polling interval.
  - After each call, the **Polling Stop Condition** code is evaluated. If it returns `true`, polling stops.
  - The polling logic can also update the action status:
    - `@status` — accepts `STATUS_COMPLETE`, `STATUS_INPROGRESS`, or `STATUS_FAILED`.
    - `@status_details` — adds a message to the Work Order view.

- - This code runs once at the end of execution or after the final poll.

  - It processes the final API response.

  - Use it to populate output variables, for example:

        @outputs['variable_name'] = value

#### Configuration Instructions

1.  Create a new action step using the **SoftlayerApi** plugin.
2.  Fill out the following fields:
    - : The name used to identify a saved **SoftlayerApi** configured instance.
    - : Some comments about this saved **SoftlayerApi** configured instance.
    - : Choose between the public or private Softlayer URL.
    - : HTTP Proxy to reach Softlayer URL.
    - : Verify SSL certificate presented by Softlayer URL.
    - : Softlayer API username.
    - : Softlayer API key.
    - : One of the Softlayer services.
    - : Softlayer initialisation parameter, which usually refers to a specific object ID.
    - : A method applicable for Softlayer service selected
    - : HTTP REST method.
    - : Softlayer response format, either XML, JSON or TXT.
    - : A list of attributes to pass as objectMask to Softlayer API.
    - : A Json string containing a Softlayer objectFilter.
    - : HTTP POST body.
    - : Timeout given to execute the request. 0 applies a 60 second default.
    - : Makes the plugin act as a continuous trigger.
    - : How often to call the URL. 0 disables polling.
    - : Ruby code that will be evaluated at every poll. Returning true stops the poll.
    - : A list of variables to make available as outputs.
    - : Ruby code that is executed at the end to fill processed outputs with values
    - : Softlayer API result limit
    - : Softlayer API result offset

#### Example Configuration

This is a minimum setup configuration for the **SoftlayerApi** plugin.

| Parameter            | Value                               |
|----------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Softlayer URL        | https://api.softlayer.com/rest/v3.1 |
| SSL verification     | Selected                            |
| Username             | admin                               |
| APIKey               | password                            |
| Softlayer Service    | SoftLayer_Account                   |
| Softlayer init param | 123456                              |
| Softlayer Method     | getObject                           |
| Rest method          | get                                 |
| Response type        | json                                |
| Continue Monitoring  | Selected                            |

#### Saved Parameters Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| **Name** | The name used to identify a saved **SoftlayerApi** configured instance. |
| **Comments** | Some comments about this saved **SoftlayerApi** configured instance. |
| **Softlayer URL** | Choose between the public or private Softlayer URL. |
| **Proxy Address** | HTTP Proxy to reach Softlayer URL. |
| **SSL verification** | Verify SSL certificate presented by Softlayer URL. |
| **Username** | Softlayer API username. |
| **APIKey** | Softlayer API key. |
| **Softlayer Service** | One of the Softlayer services. |
| **Softlayer init param** | Softlayer initialisation parameter, which usually refers to a specific object ID. |
| **Softlayer Method** | A method applicable for Softlayer service selected |
| **Rest method** | HTTP REST method. |
| **Response type** | Softlayer response format, either XML, JSON or TXT. |
| **Object Mask** | A list of attributes to pass as objectMask to Softlayer API. |
| **Object Filter** | A Json string containing a Softlayer objectFilter. |
| **Post body** | HTTP POST body. |
| **Request Timeout** | Timeout given to execute the request. 0 applies a 60 second default. |
| **Continue Monitoring** | Makes the plugin act as a continuous trigger. |
| **Polling Frequency** | How often to call the URL. 0 disables polling. |
| **Polling stop condition** | Ruby code that will be evaluated at every poll. Returning true stops the poll. |
| **Processed outputs** | A list of variables to make available as outputs. |
| **Processed outputs code** | Ruby code that is executed at the end to fill processed outputs with values |
| **Result limit** | Softlayer API result limit |
| **Result offset** | Softlayer API result offset |

#### Inputs Description

The list of inputs depends on the configuration of the **SoftlayerApi** action template. If dynamic attributes are used in the message definition, each attribute becomes a required input.

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| *Softlayer_method (mandatory)* | A method applicable for Softlayer service selected. |
| *Softlayer_initparam* | Softlayer initialisation parameter, which usually refers to a specific object ID. |
| *Object_filter* | A Json string containing a Softlayer objectFilter. |
| *Object_mask* | A list of attributes to pass as objectMask to Softlayer API. |
| *Post_body* | HTTP Post. |
| *Proxy_address* | HTTP Proxy to reach Softlayer URL. |
| *Request_timeout* | Timeout given to execute the request. 0 applies a 60 second default. |
| *Result_limit* | Softlayer API result limit. |
| *Result_offset* | Softlayer API result offset. |

#### Outputs Description

| Parameter        | Description                                       |
|------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| HTTP_return_code | The HTTP status code of the response (e.g., 200). |
| Service_result   | The result returned by the API call.              |

#### Supported Actions

None

#### Dependencies

None

#### Operating Instructions

The best place to begin is the [Softlayer API](https://sldn.softlayer.com/article/rest/) documentation. The API uses a single, fixed endpoint that cannot be changed. To authenticate, you must provide a SoftLayer username and API key, referred to in IBM Cloud as the "Classic Infrastructure API Key." A full list of available services is available in the [Softlayer Developer Network](https://sldn.softlayer.com/reference/softlayerapi/).

If the polling frequency is set above zero, the API URL is called repeatedly at the specified interval. After each call, the polling stop condition code is evaluated, and polling stops if the condition returns `true`. During polling, the step’s outcome can be controlled by updating `@status`—which may be set to `STATUS_COMPLETE`, `STATUS_INPROGRESS`, or `STATUS_FAILED`—and `@status_details`, which adds informational text to the Work Order view.

At the end of execution, or after the final poll, the processed outputs code is run. This code operates on the final API response and should be used to populate your defined output variables, for example: `@outputs['variable_name'] = value`.

### 3PlayMedia Operation

![3PlayMedia Operation icon](icons/ThreePlayOperation.png){.plugin-icon}

Add description here

| Version | Comment                                             |
|---------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| 0.1.0   | Updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8 |
| 0.0.2   | Fix autofill bug that displays username/password    |
| 0.0.1   | Initial implementation                              |

- ### Description

  #### Saved Parameters Description

- **Name**: Name of the plugin instance

- **Comments**: comments associated with the instance

- **Service URL**: URL of the 3PlayMedia service

- **API Key**: The api key to access 3PlayMedia service

- **Trigger Operation**: 1.Submit to 3Play, 2. Check Status, 3.Download Caption are the 3 operations supported in the plugin as of now

- **API secret key**: Required for Submit operation. The secret key to initiate a job

- **Link**: Link of the url used to trigger a job

- **Name 3 Play**: Name to be associated with the new job

- **Description**: Description for the job

- **File**: The file for triggering a job

- **Video**: video id for the job

- **Batch name**: Folder name for the job

- **Batch id**: Folder id associated for the job

- **Project Name**: Name of the project under which the job has to be created

- **Account Name**: Name of the account under which the job has to be created

- **Attribute1**:

- **Attribute2**:

- **Attribute3**:

- **Monitor Status for completion?**: On a submit job, once the job is submitted, check this option if you want orchestrator to monitor the status of the job for completion

- **Check for downloading caption**: Once the job is completed, if this option is checked, we initiate the download caption option

- **Polling Frequency**: For async jobs, the interval of polling

- **Starting timecode sample**: For download caption, use this option if needed

- **Frames per second**: For download caption, enter the frames per second

- **Drop Frame**:

- **Format**: Enter the format of the caption file

<!-- -->

- **service_url, apikey, spi secret key, link**: the mandatory parameters for submit job operation
- **internal_id and apikey**: the mandatory parameters for the poll for status operation
- **internal_id, apikey and format**: the mandatory parameters for the download caption operation

##### Outputs description

- Document all available outputs using add_output_help
- **Status**: Status of the job
- **Internal_id**: InternalId of the job
- **Raw Output**: Raw output received from the server
- **Error**: If any error found, this variable will hold the message

##### Operating Instructions

### Watson Language Translator

![Watson Language Translator icon](icons/WatsonLanguageTranslator.png){.plugin-icon}

This action plug-in provides the ability to translate from one language to another using IBM Watson Language Translator service.

| Version | Comment                                             |
|---------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| 0.1.0   | Updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8 |
| 0.0.1   | Initial implementation                              |

- ### Description

  #### Saved Parameters Description

- **Name**: The name used to identify a saved WatsonLanguageTranslator configured instance.

- **Comments**: Some comments about this saved WatsonLanguageTranslator configured instance.

- **Operation**: The operation you want to perform using IBM Watson Language Translator service, which are:
  - Translate - Translates text or an array of texts from one language to another.
  - List identifiable languages - Get the list of all the languages which IBM Watson Language Translator service can recognize.
  - Identify Language - Identify the language of the input text.
  - List Models - Get the list of all the language translation models provided by the IBM Watson Language Translator service.
  - Translate Document - Translate a given document from one language to another.
  - Get Document Status - Get the status of the document submitted for translation.
  - Get Transalated Document - Retrieve the translated document.

- **Url**: The URL of IBM Watson Language Translator service.By default it is https://gateway.watsonplatform.net/language-translator/api

- **Use api key**: If selected, the API key is used instead of username and password.

- **Api key**: The API key of IBM Watson Language Translator service.

- **Username**: The username of IBM Watson Language Translator service.

- **Password**: The password of IBM Watson Language Translator service.

- **Version**: The version of IBM Watson Language Translator service to be used for making API requests.By default it is 2018-05-01

- **Source Language**: Translation source language code.

- **Target Language**: Translation target language code.

- **Input text**: For Translation operation: It is the Input text in UTF-8 encoding which is required to be transalated.It can take array of inupts, Multiple entries will result in multiple translations in the response. For multiple entries the input should be in this format: \["text1","text2"\]. For Identify Language operation: It is just input text in UTF-8 format.

- **Model id**: The identifier of the model to be used for the translation request. Example the model id for translation from English to Spanish is en-es

- **File path**: The path to the file that needs to be transalated.

- **Filename**: The name of the file that needs to be transalated.

- **Document id**: The document ID of the document whose details we are trying to fetch.

- **Accept**: The type of response we want to get when trying to fetch the Translated Document. Example : text/html,text/json,text/plain,text/richtext,text/rtf,text/xml etc.

##### Input description

The list of inputs depends on the configuration of the WatsonLanguageTranslator action template.

##### Outputs description

- **Error Message**: If there is an error, this variable shows the error
- **Result**: Generally displays the Hash result for your query
- **Translations**: Displays the list of translation output in UTF-8, corresponding to the input text entries.
- **Document_id**: System generated ID identifying a document being translated using one specific translation model.
- **Filename**: Displays the filename from the submission
- **Status**: Displays the status of the translation job associated with a submitted document. Possible values: \[processing, available, failed\]

##### Operating Instructions

The IBM Watson Language Translator service is an IBM Cloud service. You get the credentials (URL, username, password, API key) with an online subscription. Using those credentials you can exceute the IBM Watson Language Translator service operations through the plugin.

## Other Utilities

### Collection manager

![Collection manager icon](icons/CollectionManager.png){.plugin-icon}

This action plug-in enables creating a name-value pair collection (Hash) or adding fields to an existing one.

| Version | Comment |
|----|----|
| 0.3.1 | Updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8 |
| 0.3.0 | Remove hardcoded aspera directory reference |
| 0.2.2 | Updated path to runtime version of ruby |
| 0.2.1 | fixed plugin description |
| 0.2.0 | added pretty JSON and TOML outputs |
| 0.1.0 | \- Marc - 2015/05/06 - Adds option to return as json and to extract specific fields |
| 0.0.1 | \- Marc - 2015/05/05 - initial release |

### How It Works

- Creates or updates a name–value pair collection (`Hash`).
- If no source is provided, a new collection is created.
- If a Hash or JSON collection is provided, elements are updated when matching keys exist or added when missing.
- Elements are always typed as defined at design-time.
- Collections can also be exported in multiple formats: JSON, Pretty JSON, or TOML.
- Specific elements can be extracted from the collection and returned as their own typed outputs.

### Configuration Instructions

1.  Create a new action step using the **CollectionManager** plug-in.
2.  Fill out the following fields:
    - **Required elements**: Mandatory elements to be added to the collection. Must be typed.
    - **Optional elements**: Optional elements that may be included, also typed.
    - **Extracted outputs**: Specific elements to return as separate typed outputs.
    - **Use JSON?**: If checked, collection is also returned serialized in JSON.
    - **Use pretty JSON?**: If checked, collection is serialized as pretty JSON.
    - **Use TOML?**: If checked, collection is serialized in TOML format.
3.  Optional: You can provide a `Source_collection` (Hash) or `Source_JSON_collection` (JSON text) to extend or modify an existing collection.

### Example Configuration

| Field             | Value                                          |
|-------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| Required elements | `Name` (String), `Age` (Integer), `DOB` (Date) |
| Optional elements | `Working` (Boolean), `Skills` (Array)          |
| Use JSON?         | Checked                                        |
| Use pretty JSON?  | Checked                                        |
| Use TOML?         | Checked                                        |

**Behavior:** This configuration builds a collection with required and optional elements. The resulting collection is returned as a Hash, JSON, Pretty JSON, and TOML string. Extracted outputs are also returned in their declared types.

### Saved Parameters Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| **Required elements** | Mandatory typed elements to include in the collection |
| **Optional elements** | Optional typed elements that may be added |
| **Extracted outputs** | Elements extracted from the collection as individual outputs |
| **Use JSON?** | Returns the collection serialized in JSON format |
| **Use pretty JSON?** | Returns the collection serialized in human-readable JSON |
| **Use TOML?** | Returns the collection serialized in TOML format |

### Inputs Description

- **Source_collection**: Optional Hash collection to use as a base. If not provided, a new collection is created.
- **Source_JSON_collection**: Optional JSON collection to use as a base. If both Hash and JSON are provided, they are merged.

All declared elements are then added or updated in the collection. Element types are enforced at design-time.

### Outputs Description

- **Modified_collection:** The new collection that is either based on the one provided or created, with the addition of all the elements defined with their provided values.
- **Modified_JSON_collection:** (Only provided if 'Use JSON?' is checked. The new collection above, but serialized in JSON and provided as plain text.)
- **Modified_pretty_JSON_collection:** (Only provided if 'Use pretty JSON?' is checked. The new collection above, but serialized in pretty JSON and provided as plain text.)
- **Modified_TOML_collection:** (Only provided if 'Use TOML?' is checked. The new collection above, but serialized in TOML and provided as plain text.)

### Supported Actions

None

### Dependencies

For Orchestrator versions earlier than 4.0.0, install the following gems for TOML support:

    gem 'citrus', '3.0.2'
    gem 'toml-rb', '2.0.1'
    bundle install

### Operating Instructions

The CollectionManager plug-in is used to build or update typed collections. You can merge collections, enforce typing, and output in multiple formats. It is especially useful for workflows where structured data needs to be passed across steps.

### Troubleshooting

- Ensure all element types match the declared type; no implicit conversion occurs.
- Verify that provided JSON is valid when using `Source_JSON_collection`.
- If TOML output fails on Orchestrator \< 4.0.0, confirm that `citrus` and `toml-rb` gems are installed.
- Check that the extracted output names match the elements in the collection.

### Custom Python

![Custom Python icon](icons/CustomPython.png){.plugin-icon}

This action plug-in can be used to script in python for some customized behaviour.

| Version | Comment |
|----|----|
| 0.1.2 | Log errors to stderr instead of displaying stacktrace in work order output |
| 0.1.1 | Add Bitbucket integration |
| 0.1.0 | Updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8 |
| 0.0.2 | Fix Help |
| 0.0.1 | Initial Release. |

### How It Works

- This plugin allows execution of Python code within the workflows.
- It enables dynamic logic, data transformation, and integration with external systems.
- The plugin uses declared inputs and outputs to interact with the workflow and must explicitly set the workflow status.

### Configuration Instructions

1.  `Add a `**`Custom Python`**` step to your workflow.`
2.  `Configure the execution environment:`
    - **`Name`**`: The label to identify this ConvertPython step.`
    - **`Required inputs`**`: Define the inputs your Python code needs.`
    - **`Outputs`**`: Define the expected outputs from the script.`
    - **`Execute code`**`: Write or paste the Python logic to be executed.`

### Example Configuration

#### Example 1: Sample Python code to return a file size.

\

| Field               | Parameter | Type    |
|---------------------|-----------|---------|
| **Required inputs** | file_path | String  |
| **Outputs**         | file_size | Integer |

\

    import os

    file_path = inputs["file_path"]

    if os.path.isfile(file_path):

      outputs["file_size"]  = os.path.getsize(file_path)

      outputs["status"] = "Complete"

    else:

      outputs["status"] = "Failed"

#### Example 2: Sample code to parse XML data.

\

| Field               | Parameter       | Type   |
|---------------------|-----------------|--------|
| **Required inputs** | file_path       | String |
| **Outputs**         | media_file_name | String |

\
`The XML file is:`

    <Asset>

      <File>The-Hobbit-VO.mxf</File>

      <Profile>XDCAM HD422</Profile>

      <Target>VOD Distrib</Target>

      <Source>Internal Demo</Source>

    </Asset>

\
\
`The Python code is:`

    import os

    import xmltodict

    xml_file_path = inputs['file_path']

    with open(xml_file_path) as fd:

        data = xmltodict.parse(fd.read())

    try:

        outputs['media_file_name'] = data['Asset']['File']

    except:

        pass

\
\
`Note: inputs['var_name'] can also be abbreviated var_name. Example:`

    xml_file_path = file_path

\
\
`To specify a custom status message, you can set the status_details key of the outputs hash with the customized message using this syntax:`

    outputs['status_details'] = 'Customized status details message'

### Saved Parameters Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| **Name** | The label to identify this ConvertPython step. |
| **Required inputs** | Define the inputs your Python code needs. |
| **Optional inputs** | Add any additional inputs that may or may not be used. |
| **Outputs** | Define the expected outputs from the script. |
| **Execute code** | Write or paste the Python logic to be executed. |
| **Use code from source control?** | If you do not want to enter the code inline, enable this option to fetch it from a source control system. Use the dropdown to select either **GitHub** or **Bitbucket**. The available fields below will update based on your selection. See the *Source Control Configuration* section for details. |
| **Use cached code?** | If checked, the plugin uses the last saved (cached) version of the Python code instead of downloading the latest version from source control every time the step runs. This helps keep workflow runs consistent, especially when the remote code is being updated. If the cached code is missing, the plugin will automatically fetch the latest code and update the cache. |
| **Verify SSL** | Check this box to use SSL when connecting to the source control host. |

### Source Control Configuration

When **Use code from source control?** is enabled, select a provider from the dropdown. The fields displayed will vary depending on the selected provider.

#### GitHub

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| **GitHub host name** | If you are using GitHub Enterprise, enter the host name used to access your GitHub Enterprise instance. |
| **GitHub token** | For private repositories, enter the personal access token of the GitHub user for authentication. |
| **GitHub repo** | The repository name where the Python code file resides. |
| **GitHub file path** | The path to the file from the root of the repository (exclude the repository name itself). |
| **GitHub branch name** | The branch to fetch the code from. |
| **GitHub user** | The GitHub username under which the repository is present. |

\
The GitHub API URL format used is: *https://api.github.com/repos/Username/RepositoryName/contents/FilePath?ref=BranchName*

#### Bitbucket

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| **Bitbucket host name** | Enter the Bitbucket host name in API URL format (e.g. *api.bitbucket.org* for standard Bitbucket Cloud, or your self-hosted equivalent) |
| **Bitbucket token** | For private repositories, enter the access token or app password for authentication. |
| **Bitbucket repo** | The repository name where the Python code file resides. |
| **Bitbucket file path** | The path to the file from the root of the repository (exclude the repository name itself). |
| **Bitbucket branch name** | The branch to fetch the code from. |
| **Workspace** | The Bitbucket workspace under which the repository is present. |

\
If the source control host is unreachable, the plugin falls back to a cached version of the code stored at: */opt/aspera/orchestrator/config/orchestrator/custom_pythons*

### Inputs Description

- The list of inputs depends on the configuration of the CustomPython action template.
- The inputs for the action will be the ones declared in the 'Required inputs' and 'Optional inputs' section.

### Outputs Description

- The outputs optionally declared in the 'Outputs' section will be returned with their specified names and types.

### Supported Actions

None

### Operating Instructions

- The plugin allows running Python code.
- Inputs are accessed using the **inputs** dictionary.
- The Flask server will run on port 3200 on enabling the plugin (by default). To customize that port, customers can change the port specified */opt/aspera/orchestrator/actions/custom_pythons/orchestrator-custom-pythons_flask_server.service*. After that, they will need to **disable the plugin and then re-enable it**.
- The Flask server will stop on disabling the plugin.
- Inputs must be declared under Required or Optional inputs.
- Outputs are accessed using the **outputs** dictionary.
- Two reserved output keys are status and status_details.
- To update workflow status, use two reserved output keys: status and status_details.
- Both status and status_details must be part of the outputs dictionary.
- The code must set outputs\['status'\] to one of the following values: '*Complete*' – when the workflow finishes successfully, '*Failed*' – when the workflow fails and '*Error*' – when there is an execution error
- The plugin includes source control integration to fetch code directly from GitHub or Bitbucket repositories.
- When GitHub/Bitbucket parameters are provided as mapped inputs, the UI will display them as GitHub parameters (for example, Github_token), even if Bitbucket is configured. Bitbucket authentication will still function correctly.
- If the source control host is unreachable, the plugin uses a cached version of the code.
- Cached code is stored at: */opt/aspera/orchestrator/config/orchestrator/custom_pythons*

### Dependencies

- Python3, Flask and flask_cors must be installed in the same environment as the Orchestrator server.
- A Flask server file is available at: */opt/aspera/orchestrator/actions/custom_pythons/flask_server.py*
- This file can be used to run the Flask server required for the plugin if the server is not running already.

#### Installing Flask and Dependencies

**For RHEL 9 and Rocky Linux 9:**

```

yum install pip
pip install flask
pip install flask_cors
```

**For RHEL 8 and Rocky Linux 8:**

```

dnf install python3-pip
pip3 install flask
pip3 install flask_cors
```

### Hello world

![Hello world icon](icons/HelloWorld.png){.plugin-icon}

This is a reference implementation for a simple Action plugin illustrating the API.

| Version | Comment                                                 |
|---------|---------------------------------------------------------|
| 1.3.0   | Made fixes to ensure that the plugin runs to completion |
| 1.2.0   | Updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8     |
| 1.1.0   | Initial release                                         |

#### How It Works

- Constructs a **customizable message** by parsing a prompt string at execution time.
- Supports **variable substitution** using the syntax `<%=variable_name%>` embedded in static text.
- Displays the message **letter by letter** with a configurable pause between each character.
- Default tempo is **2 seconds per letter**, overridable at runtime.
- Prompt can be stored in the database or **provided at runtime** via optional input.
- Runtime prompt takes **precedence** over stored prompt if both are provided.
- Supports workflow actions: **Pause, Resume, Cancel, and Rollback**.

#### Configuration Instructions

1.  Create a new action step using the **HelloWorld** plugin.
2.  Fill out the following fields:
    - : The name used to identify a saved HelloWorld configured instance.
    - : Comments about this saved HelloWorld configured instance.
    - : The string to use as a prompt. That string can include variables that get replaced during execution by their run-time values.

#### Example Configuration

This is an example configuration for the HelloWorld plugin with variable substitution.

| Parameter | Value                                             |
|-----------|---------------------------------------------------|
| Name      | Welcome Message                                   |
| Comments  | Displays a personalized greeting message          |
| Prompt    | Hello \<%=name%\>, welcome to \<%=system_name%\>! |

#### Saved Parameters Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| **Name** | The name used to identify a saved HelloWorld configured instance. |
| **Comments** | Comments about this saved HelloWorld configured instance. |
| **Prompt** | The string to use as a prompt. That string can include variables that get replaced during execution by their run-time values. |

#### Inputs Description

Both inputs are optional. The action can execute using only the stored prompt with default tempo settings.

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| *prompt_override* | Provides the ability to use a run-time variable to set the prompt pattern instead of using a value stored in the database. If both are provided, the run-time one takes precedence. |
| *tempo* | Provides the ability to use a different value for the duration of the pause between each letter during execution. Expressed in seconds. Default is 2 seconds. |

#### Outputs Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| message | A string representing the prompt provided with the variables replaced by their values at run-time |

#### Supported Actions

- **Pause:** Temporarily halts the letter-by-letter display. The message remains partially displayed.
- **Resume:** Continues the letter-by-letter display from where it was paused.
- **Cancel:** Stops the action execution immediately. The partial message is not saved.
- **Rollback:** Reverses the action execution. Can be used to undo the message display in workflow rollback scenarios.

#### Dependencies

None

#### Operating Instructions

This action returns a string that represents a message constructed at execution time by parsing the prompt (either stored in the database, or provided at execution time using the prompt_override optional input variable).

**Variable Syntax and Substitution\**
The prompt string can contain static text and variables expanded at run-time. Variables are indicated using the syntax **\<%=variable_name%\>** embedded into static text.

- **Basic variable substitution:** `"Hello <%=name%>."`
- If `name = "John"`, the output becomes: `"Hello John."`
- **Multiple variables:** `"Welcome <%=first_name%> <%=last_name%> to <%=company%>!"`
- If `first_name = "Jane"`, `last_name = "Doe"`, `company = "Acme Corp"`, the output becomes: `"Welcome Jane Doe to Acme Corp!"`
- **System variables:** `"Processing started at <%=timestamp%> on <%=hostname%>"`
- Variables can reference any input, output, or system variable available in the workflow context.

**Prompt Configuration Options\**

- **Stored Prompt (Database):**
  - Configure the prompt in the 'Prompt' parameter during action template creation
  - Prompt is saved in the database and reused for all executions
  - Best for static messages that rarely change
  - Example: `"System backup completed successfully."`
- **Runtime Prompt Override:**
  - Provide the prompt using the `prompt_override` optional input at execution time
  - Runtime prompt takes precedence over stored prompt
  - Best for dynamic messages that vary per execution
  - Example: Different messages based on workflow conditions or external data
- **Hybrid Approach:**
  - Configure a default prompt in the database
  - Override with runtime prompt only when needed
  - Provides flexibility while maintaining a fallback default

**Tempo Configuration\**

- The **tempo** controls the speed at which the message is displayed letter by letter.
- **Default tempo:** 2 seconds per letter
- **Fast display:** Set tempo to 0.1 or 0.5 seconds for quick message rendering
- **Slow display:** Set tempo to 5 or 10 seconds for dramatic effect or demonstration purposes
- **Instant display:** Set tempo to 0 to display the entire message immediately
- Tempo can be overridden at runtime using the `tempo` optional input (expressed in seconds).

**Common Use Cases\**

- **Welcome message:** Greet users by name when they log in or start a workflow.
- **Status notification:** Display dynamic status messages with variable information (e.g., file count, processing time).
- **Demonstration/Training:** Show slow letter-by-letter display for educational purposes.
- **Workflow logging:** Generate formatted log messages with runtime variable values.
- **Conditional messaging:** Use runtime prompt override to display different messages based on workflow conditions.

**Best Practices\**

- Use **descriptive variable names** in prompts for better readability and maintainability.
- Test prompts with sample data to ensure variables are replaced correctly.
- Set appropriate **tempo values** based on use case: fast for production, slow for demonstrations.
- Use **stored prompts** for static messages and **runtime override** for dynamic content.
- Ensure all referenced variables are available in the workflow context before execution.
- Consider using **Pause/Resume** actions for long messages that may need interruption.
- Document variable requirements in the Comments field for easier template maintenance.

**Example Execution Flow\**

**Configuration:**\
Prompt (stored): `"Processing file <%=filename%> for user <%=username%>."`\
Tempo: 2 seconds (default)\
\
**Runtime Variables:**\
`filename = "report.pdf"`\
`username = "admin"`\
\
**Execution:**\
The action displays the message letter by letter:\
`P` (wait 2s) `r` (wait 2s) `o` (wait 2s) `c` (wait 2s) ... `Processing file report.pdf for user admin.`\
\
**Output:**\
`message = "Processing file report.pdf for user admin."`

### Input Manipulations

![Input Manipulations icon](icons/InputManipulation.png){.plugin-icon}

This action plug-in can be used to manipulate inputs using data operations based on the input type.

| Version | Comment                                             |
|---------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| 0.1.0   | Updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8 |
| 0.0.2   | Bug fix. Allows array and hash as run time inputs   |
| 0.0.1   | initial release                                     |

#### How It Works

- Provides a comprehensive set of **data manipulation operations** for strings, arrays, and hashes.
- Supports **string operations**: split, compare, substitute (sub), global substitute (gsub), and more.
- Supports **array operations**: concatenation and type conversion.
- Supports **hash operations**: key-value retrieval and type conversion.
- Allows **pattern matching** using both strings and regular expressions.
- Returns processed output based on the selected operation and input type.
- Enables **dynamic data transformation** within workflows without custom scripting.

#### Configuration Instructions

1.  Create a new action step using the **InputManipulation** plugin.
2.  Fill out the following fields:
    - : The name used to identify a saved Input Manipulation configured instance.
    - : Comments about this saved Input Manipulation configured instance.
    - : Select the type of input - String, Array, or Hash.
    - : The operation to be performed on the input based on the selected input type.
    - : Inputs that must be provided for the selected operation to execute.
    - : Additional inputs that may be required depending on the specific operation chosen.
    - : The output variables that will be returned after the operation is performed.

#### Example Configuration

This is an example configuration for the InputManipulation plugin performing a string split operation.

| Parameter  | Value                           |
|------------|---------------------------------|
| Name       | String Split Operation          |
| Comments   | Split email address by @ symbol |
| Input type | String                          |
| Operation  | Split                           |
| Input      | user@example.com                |
| Pattern    | '@'                             |

#### Saved Parameters Description

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| **Name** | The name used to identify a saved Input Manipulation configured instance. |
| **Comments** | Comments about this saved Input Manipulation configured instance. |
| **Input type** | Select the type of input - String, Array, or Hash. |
| **Operation** | The operation to be performed on the input based on the selected input type. |
| **Required inputs** | Inputs that must be provided for the selected operation to execute. |
| **Optional inputs** | Additional inputs that may be required depending on the specific operation chosen. |
| **Outputs** | The output variables that will be returned after the operation is performed. |

#### Inputs Description

The inputs for the action will be the ones declared in the 'Required inputs' and 'Optional inputs' section. The specific inputs required depend on the selected input type and operation.

**General Inputs\**

| Parameter    | Description                                    |
|--------------|------------------------------------------------|
| *Input type* | Select the type of input - String, Array, Hash |
| *Operation*  | The operation to be performed on the input     |

**String Operations - Additional Inputs\**

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| *Input* | The input string on which the selected operation is performed |
| *Pattern(String/Regexp)* | This input is required only for Split, Compare, Sub and Gsub operations. If string is used, enter it within quotes. Pattern can also be given as Regexp |
| *Replace by* | This input is required only for Sub and Gsub operations |

**Array Operations - Additional Inputs\**

| Parameter | Description                                                  |
|-----------|--------------------------------------------------------------|
| *Array1*  | The input array on which the selected operation is performed |
| *Array2*  | This input is required only for Concat operation             |

**Hash Operations - Additional Inputs\**

| Parameter | Description                                                     |
|-----------|-----------------------------------------------------------------|
| *Input*   | The input hash on which the selected operation is performed     |
| *Key*     | This input is required only for 'Get Value for a Key' operation |

#### Outputs Description

The outputs optionally declared in the 'Outputs' section will be returned with their specified names and types.

| Parameter | Description |
|----|----|
| output | The processed output based on the operation performed. The type and content depend on the input type and operation selected |

#### Supported Actions

None

#### Dependencies

None

#### Operating Instructions

This plug-in provides comprehensive data manipulation operations for strings, arrays, and hashes.

**Available String Operations\**

- **Split** - Divides a string into an array based on a pattern (string or regexp). Requires: Input, Pattern.
- **Compare** - Compares a string with a pattern and returns true/false. Requires: Input, Pattern.
- **Sub** - Substitutes the first occurrence of a pattern with a replacement string. Requires: Input, Pattern, Replace by.
- **Gsub** - Globally substitutes all occurrences of a pattern with a replacement string. Requires: Input, Pattern, Replace by.
- **Upcase** - Converts all characters in the string to uppercase. Requires: Input.
- **Downcase** - Converts all characters in the string to lowercase. Requires: Input.
- **Capitalize** - Capitalizes the first character of the string. Requires: Input.

**Available Array Operations\**

- **Concat** - Concatenates two arrays into a single array. Requires: Array1, Array2.
- **ConvertToString** - Converts the given array into a string. Requires: Array1.
- **ConvertToHash** - Converts the given array into a hash. Requires: Array1.

**Available Hash Operations\**

- **Get Value for a Key** - Retrieves the value associated with a specific key. Requires: Input, Key.
- **Get Keys** - Returns an array of all keys in the hash. Requires: Input.
- **Get Values** - Returns an array of all values in the hash. Requires: Input.
- **Hash to Array** - Converts the given hash into an array. Requires: Input.

**Common Use Cases and Examples\**

- **Email domain extraction:** Use Split operation with pattern `"@"` on input `"user@example.com"` to get `["user", "example.com"]`
- **Text normalization:** Use Gsub operation to replace multiple spaces with single space, pattern `/\s+/`, replace by `" "`
- **Array merging:** Use Concat operation to combine two arrays, e.g., `[1,2,3]` + `[4,5,6]` = `[1,2,3,4,5,6]`
- **Configuration lookup:** Use Get Value for a Key operation to retrieve values from configuration hashes
- **Data validation:** Use Compare operation to validate string patterns, e.g., checking if an email contains `"@"`

**Best Practices\**

- Choose the appropriate input type (String, Array, Hash) before selecting operations.
- Use regular expressions for complex pattern matching instead of simple strings when needed.
- Test patterns with sample data to ensure correct matching behavior.
- Use Gsub instead of Sub when you need to replace all occurrences of a pattern.
- Validate that required inputs are provided based on the selected operation.
- Chain multiple Input Manipulation steps for complex transformations.
- Use descriptive output variable names for better workflow readability.

### Stored parameter set

![Stored parameter set icon](icons/MassParameterSetter.png){.plugin-icon}

This action plug-in provides the ability to set the values of multiple parameters within a workflow step. It can be used to store default values or apply a predefined set of values for reuse across workflows. This helps reduce duplication and ensures consistency when configuring actions.

| Version | Comment |
|----|----|
| 0.6.3 | Make parameter field columns expandable |
| 0.6.2 | Add support for password type parameters |
| 0.6.1 | Fix issues when storing certain string characters (Like colons) |
| 0.6.0 | Updating to be compatible with Ruby 3.4 and Rails 8 |
| 0.5.6 | Fixes issue with gsub in input |
| 0.5.5 | Fixes issue when removing a single parameter |
| 0.5.4 | Fixes dynamic parameters with single quotes |
| 0.5.3 | Fixes configurator UI affecting Orchestrator 3+ deployment using ruby 2.3 preventing the use of dynamic attributes |
| 0.5.2 | Ensures that stored YML can be viewed even if escaped characters are present |
| 0.5.1 | Added backward capability to view external stored YML |
| 0.5.0 | Added capability to view external stored YML |
| 0.4.3 | Fixes odd issue for windows path finishing with \\ |
| 0.4.2 | Enable safe export/import when using external data source |
| 0.4.1 | Fixes typo in editor, rename JS function to avoid collisions |
| 0.4.0 | New icon |
| 0.3.9 | Added examples for the two YAML formats when using an external configuration file |
| 0.3.8 | Fixes naming pop-up triggering |
| 0.3.7 | Added support for Array and Hash |
| 0.3.6 | Matching outputs hash to the outputs spec for external parameters file |
| 0.3.5 | Added extra condition for when the YAML file is not Marshalled |
| 0.3.4 | Added more verbiage in help file for usage of external configuration file |
| 0.3.3 | Prevents the use of special characters for parameter names in the configurator screen |
| 0.3.2 | Fixed problems with windows paths with / as last character |
| 0.3.1 | Fixed problems with windows paths and single quote characters. Added support for dynamic inputs. |
| 0.3.0 | Increase the DB size for the parameter length. Added Help file |
| 0.2.1 | Fixes non-external storage output definition |
| 0.2.0 | Adds external storage option |
| 0.1.3 | Extracts read and write from the execute method. |
| 0.1.2 | Adds display name |
| 0.1.1 | fixed template layout |
| 0.1.0 | initial version |

### How It Works

- Centralizes workflow configuration in one place, similar to a configuration file.
- Allows defining any number of parameters that will be exposed in the workflow using their names.
